claims.claim: At the separator collapsing temperature Tsc, the partial pressure of gas generated by redox reactions (Pg) is nearly 3922.9 kPa, which is over six times larger than the electrolyte vapor pressure (PELE).
claims.quote: At the temperature point of Tsc, Pg is nearly 3922.9 kPa, which is over six times larger than PELE.

claims.claim: At the venting temperature of 405.5 K for an intact 100% SOC NCM811 battery cell, the gas pressure from redox reactions (Pg = 995.79 kPa) is nearly three times larger than the electrolyte vapor pressure (PELE = 302.42 kPa), making Pg the main driver of safety valve bursting.
claims.quote: the venting temperature is 405.5 K with Pg and PELE corresponding to 995.79 kPa and 302.42 kPa respectively. The pressure of the gas generated by the redox reaction bears the main responsibility for the safety valve bursting, which is nearly three times larger than the vapor pressure of electrolyte.

claims.claim: The calculated total pressure inside the intact battery cell at venting was 1298.21 kPa, which lies within the manufacturer-specified reasonable pressure range of 1.3 MPa to 1.6 MPa.
claims.quote: The calculated pressure inside the battery cell is 1298.21 kPa totally, which lied in the reasonable pressure range provided by the manufacturer from 1.3 MPa to 1.6 MPa.

claims.claim: The relationship between temperature increasing rate and gas generation rate is not linear before the separator collapsing temperature Tsc, but becomes approximately linear when temperature approaches Tsc, indicating a multi-step reaction process before Tsc and a one-step reaction near Tsc.
claims.quote: the relationship between temperature increasing rate and gas generation rate is not linear until battery temperature approaches the uncontrollable temperature... the relationship between both is approximately linear when the temperature approaches Tsc. Therefore, the one-step reaction is reasonable when the temperature approaches Tsc.

claims.claim: If the safety valve threshold pressure is set too high, gas generated during thermal runaway inside battery cells is not vented in time, resulting in commercial cell designs rupturing and exploding easily, especially at high temperatures.
claims.quote: if the threshold of the safety valve is set high, gas generation during thermal runaway inside the battery cells is not vented timely, resulting in commercial cell designs rupturing and exploding easily especially at high temperatures, presenting serious safety concerns.

claims.claim: During safety venting of NMC-811 21700 cells, methane (CH4) was consistently the most abundant gas detected across all SoC, and CH4 concentration was positively correlated with SoC.
claims.quote: During safety venting, CH4 was consistently the most abundant gas detected across all SoC. At 25% SoC, the % volume increase observed for CH4 was 5.50%, compared to 0.15% CO2, 0.02% CO, 0.35% C2H6 and 0% H2. At 100% SoC, the % volume for CH4 was 6.8% compared to 0.29% CO2, 0.01% CO, 0.45% C2H6 and 0% H2.

claims.claim: No H2 was detected during safety venting of NMC-811 21700 cells across all tested SoC levels.
claims.quote: in our tests, no H2 was detected during safety venting. A similar observation was reported by J. Siegel [34], who noted that H2 is not detected consistently in first venting events.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of NMC-811 21700 cells, H2 peak volume increased from 0.20% at 5% SoC to 12.9% at 100% SoC, showing a strong positive correlation with SoC.
claims.quote: Our data revealed a strong correlation between H2 concentration and SoC, with peak % volume increases ranging from 0.20% at 5% SoC to 12.9% at 100% SoC.

claims.claim: When SoC was increased from 25% to 100%, total gas volume produced after thermal runaway increased 3 to 5-fold in both air and nitrogen atmospheres; in air from 0.8 L to 3.9 L, and in N2 from 2.2 L to 6.5 L.
claims.quote: For both the air and N2 based tests, we observed a 3-5-fold jump in the total volume of gas produced when the SoC was increased from 25% to 100%. In air, the total gas generated increased from 0.8 L at 25% SoC to 3.9 L at 100% SoC; for the tests conducted in N2, 2.2 L of gas was produced at 25% SoC, which increased to 6.5 L at 100% SoC.

claims.claim: At 100% SoC, thermal runaway onset temperature was up to 60°C lower than at 5% SoC, and the gas detection window before thermal runaway was 120 s shorter at 100% SoC (142 s) compared to 5% SoC (262 s).
claims.quote: At 100% SoC, thermal runaway can initiate at a temperature up to 60 ◦C lower than at 5% SoC. Our observed temperature changes are supported by previous studies... At the extremes, the window of opportunity to detect vent gas prior to thermal runaway was 120 s shorter at 100% SoC (t = 142 s) when compared to 5% SoC (t = 262 s) under the same test conditions.

claims.claim: The primary gases ejected during thermal runaway of 280Ah LiFePO4 batteries are H2 (55.22%), CO2 (23.17%), and CO (7.97%), with a single cell releasing an average of 6.6301 mol of gas.
claims.quote: the primary gases ejected during thermal runaway of 280Ah LiFePO4 batteries are H2 (55.22 %), CO2 (23.17 %), and CO (7.97 %), with a single cell releasing 6.6301 mol of gas

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of two 280Ah LFP batteries in a battery pack, the pressure balance valve opened 36 times, with 72.2% of pressure relief events occurring at a rate of 0.0–0.5 kPa/s.
claims.quote: the pressure balance valve opens 36 times during the thermal runaway of two batteries, with 72.2 % of the pressure relief events occurring at a rate of 0.0–0.5 kPa/s

claims.claim: The mass of ejected gas accounted for approximately 2% of total battery mass, wreckage accounted for approximately 78%, and condensed electrolyte liquid accounted for approximately 20% after thermal runaway of a 280Ah LFP battery.
claims.quote: the mass of the ejected gas accounted for about 2 % of the total battery mass, the wreckage accounted for about 78.1 %, and the condensed electrolyte liquid accounted for about 19.9 %

claims.claim: The mass ratio of non-condensable gas to condensable liquid in the ejected substances during thermal runaway of a 280Ah LFP battery was approximately 1:10.
claims.quote: the mass ratio of gas to liquid in the ejected substances was approximately 1:10

claims.claim: The first opening of the pressure balance valve occurred at approximately 107.69–107.23 kPa, while subsequent openings occurred at approximately 105.74–106.24 kPa, both slightly above the manufacturer-specified opening pressure of 104.825 kPa.
claims.quote: the first opening occurred at approximately 107.69–107.23 kPa, while subsequent openings occurred at around 105.74–106.24 kPa. The manufacturer-specified opening pressure for the pressure balance valve was 104.825 kPa, and the experimental results were slightly higher than this reference value

claims.claim: The highest flame propagation velocity of LIB vent gases in a pipeline was about 20.4 m/s, occurring at approximately 12.5 ms during the spherical flame stage.
claims.quote: the flame velocity increased sharply to about 20.4 m/s in 12.5 ms. This corresponds to the spherical flame stage and the flame could propagate freely.

claims.claim: The highest overpressure of LIB vent gases flame propagation in a pipeline was about 0.595 MPa, which was lower than the overpressure of pure H2 or pure CH4 in air (about 0.7 MPa) due to the presence of inert gas in LIB vent gases.
claims.quote: The highest overpressure was about 0.595 MPa. This value was lower than the overpressure of pure H2 or pure CH4 in air (about 0.7 MPa), it was mainly because of the LIB vent gases including inert gas.

claims.claim: In the energy storage container explosion simulation, the maximum explosion overpressure inside the container was about 23 kPa, because the venting of pressure relief panels and escape door greatly decreased the overpressure.
claims.quote: the maximum explosion overpressure reached 23.19 kPa inside the container... The venting of pressure relief panels and door could greatly decrease the overpressure.

claims.claim: In the energy storage container explosion, the pressure relief panels opened when internal pressure reached 3 kPa, and the escape door opened when internal pressure reached 20 kPa.
claims.quote: At t = 0.0695 s, the pressure inside the box reached to 3 kPa, so the pressure relief panels opened... At t = 0.1417 s, the pressure inside the box reached 20 kPa, causing the door to open.

claims.claim: The maximum damage distance caused by explosion pressure of LIB vent gases in an energy storage container was about 10 m, based on a minimum damage threshold of 4.8 kPa overpressure.
claims.quote: Based on previous studies [46–48], 4.8 kPa was selected as the threshold for the minimum damage. The result showed that the overpressure of P8, P9 and P10 was close to 4.8 kPa, while the overpressure of P3, P4 and P5 was smaller than the threshold. It meant that the maximum distance of damage was about 10 m.

claims.claim: The thickness of the distribution of free radicals decreased with the flame propagation process in the pipeline, and lower free radical concentration corresponded to lower flame propagation velocity.
claims.quote: the thickness of the distribution of free radicals was decreasing with the flame propagation process. This corresponded to the variety of flame velocity, the lower the concentration of free radicals, the lower the flame propagation velocity.

claims.claim: At a LIB vent gas concentration of about 20%, the explosion pressure was maximized and the rate of pressure rise was also at a high level.
claims.quote: The results show that at a concentration of about 20 %, not only is the explosion pressure of the LIB vent gases maximized, but the rate of pressure rise is also at a high level.

claims.claim: The high temperature zone of LIB vent gases jet flame resulted from pyrolysis of CH3 radicals and reaction of OH radicals, with the top of the high temperature zone coinciding with OH distribution and the bottom coinciding with CH3 distribution.
claims.quote: The result indicated that the top of high temperature zone coincided with the location of OH distribution, while the bottom of high temperature zone coincided with the location of CH3 distribution. This demonstrated that the high temperature zone resulted from pyrolysis of CH3 radicals and reaction of OH radicals.

claims.claim: The explosion risk of the door side of the energy storage container was higher than that of the pressure relief panels side, because the larger area of the door allowed more gases to be vented through it.
claims.quote: the risk of door side was higher than that of the relief panels side. This is because the larger area of door, so the more gases could be vented through the door.

claims.claim: The jet flame height of LIB vent gases increased with the increase of state of charge (SOC).
claims.quote: Mao [20] also analyzed the jet flame of LIB vent gases with different state of charge (SOC) in open space and found that the height of the jet flame increased with the increase of SOC.

claims.claim: No thermal runaway propagation occurred in NCM622 battery modules when the SOC was below 25% in closed space.
claims.quote: no thermal runaway occurred in the battery module when the state of charge was below 25% in closed space.

claims.claim: When SOC decreased from 100% to 50%, the total gas production volume of NCM622 battery modules decreased from 17.48 mol to 6.17 mol.
claims.quote: The total gas production volume from 17.48 mol to 6.17 mol when the state of charge was reduced from 100% to 50%.

claims.claim: With decreasing SOC, CO2 content in vent gas increased from 38.91% to 70.45%, while CO content decreased from 23.91% to 0.06% and C2H4 content decreased from 8.92% to 1.15%.
claims.quote: as the SOC decreased, the content of CO2 increases from 38.91% to 70.45%. The content of CO and C2H4 decreased with the decrease of SOC, from 23.91% and 8.92–0.06% and 1.15% respectively.

claims.claim: When SOC decreased from 100% to 50%, the average thermal runaway propagation time between batteries in the module increased from 96.93 s to 221.88 s.
claims.quote: The average propagation times between modules at 100–50% SOC were 96.93 s, 162.85 s, and 221.88 s, respectively.

claims.claim: When SOC decreased from 100% to 25%, the internal thermal runaway propagation speed of battery #1 decreased from 4.06 mm/s to 1.15 mm/s, the maximum surface temperature decreased from 927.2°C to 486.6°C, and the mass loss rate decreased from 24.06 g/s to 8.7 g/s.
claims.quote: When the SOC decreased from 100% to 25%, the highest temperature on the battery surface decreased from 927.2 ℃ to 486.6 ℃; the speed of TRP inside the battery cells decreased from 4.06 mm⋅s−1 to 1.15 mm⋅s−1; the rate of mass loss decreased from 24.06 g/s to 8.7 g/s.

claims.claim: When SOC was higher than 50%, the explosive limit range of combustible gas generated by the NCM622 battery module was between 7.64% and 56.19%, approximately 5 times the explosive limit range of CH4.
claims.quote: when the SOC was higher than 50%, the explosive limit range of combustible gas generated by the battery module was between 7.64% and 56.19%. Comparing it with the explosive limit of flammable gas CH4, it can be found that the explosive limit range of the mixed gas generated by the module was about 5 times that of CH4

claims.claim: For a 280Ah LFP prismatic cell, TR severity intensifies above 50% SOC; during 25%, 50%, and 75% SOC tests, maximum cell temperature increased sequentially by 35% and 258.3%, while gas emissions rose by 79.3% and 644.7%, respectively.
claims.quote: Results demonstrate that TR severity intensifies above 50 % SOC. During the 25 %, 50 %, and 75 % SOC tests, the maximum cell temperature increased sequentially by 35 % and 258.3 %, while gas emissions rose by 79.3 % and 644.7 %, respectively.

claims.claim: The number of gaseous components detected during thermal runaway increases with SOC: 15 components at 25% SOC, 19 at 50% SOC, and 36 at 75% SOC.
claims.quote: Furthermore, 15, 19, and 36 gaseous components are detected under the three conditions.

claims.claim: At higher SOC, vent gas composition shifts toward reduced CO2 fraction and elevated H2 fraction.
claims.quote: At higher SOCs, a notable characteristic of the gas composition is the reduction in CO2 and the elevation in H2.

claims.claim: The lower flammability limit (LFL) for vent gases at 25% and 50% SOC (10%–15%) is significantly higher than at 75% SOC (5%), indicating greater ignition susceptibility at 75% SOC.
claims.quote: The lower flammability limit for 25 % and 50 % SOC (10 %–15 %) is slightly higher than that for 75 % SOC (5 %).

claims.claim: The Flammability Hazard Index (FHI) of vent gas mixtures follows the order 50% SOC > 75% SOC > 25% SOC, indicating that flammability risk does not increase monotonically with SOC.
claims.quote: The FHI followed the order: 50 % SOC > 75 % SOC > 25 % SOC, indicating that flammability risk does not always increase monotonically with SOC.

claims.claim: At 50% SOC, 50% SOC appears to be the critical threshold for triggering thermal runaway in the tested 280Ah LFP cells; cells at 0%, 25%, and 50% SOC did not exhibit significant TR behavior after prolonged heating, reaching Tmax of 77°C, 92.3°C, and 251.1°C respectively.
claims.quote: For all tested cells, 50 % SOC appears to be the critical threshold for triggering TR. However, this issue warrants further investigation for cells with different material systems or energy densities... the cells at 0 %, 25 %, and 50 % SOC exhibited a slight temperature increase followed by a subsequent decrease, with Tmax reaching 77 ℃, 92.3 ℃, and 251.1 ℃ at approximately 5900 s, 5122 s, and 3560 s, respectively.

claims.claim: Gas production at 120%, 100%, 75%, and 50% SOC was 16.5, 13.9, 7.4, and 2.9 times that at 25% SOC, respectively, demonstrating a strong positive relationship between SOC and total gas emission.
claims.quote: gas emissions increase with SOC, with gas production at 120 %, 100 %, 75 %, and 50 % SOC being 16.5, 13.9, 7.4, and 2.9 times that at 25 % SOC, respectively.

claims.claim: The upper flammability limit (UFL) for vent gases at 25% and 50% SOC (55%–65%) is significantly higher than at 75% SOC (4.7%–6.6%), and the flammable range (RF) at 25%, 50%, and 75% SOC are 43.7%, 48.5%, and 17.6%, respectively.
claims.quote: The upper flammability limit for 25 % and 50 % SOC (55 %–65 %) is significantly higher than that for 75 % SOC (4.7 %–6.6 %)... The RF corresponding to 25 %, 50 %, and 75 % SOC are approximately 43.7 %, 48.5 %, and 17.6 %, respectively.

claims.claim: As SOC increases from 0% to 120%, the maximum vent gas temperature increases from 100.7°C to 418.8°C, following a linear relationship described by Maxgas_temp = 2.9 × SOC + 80.75 (R² = 0.97827).
claims.quote: During tests at 0–120 % SOC, the maximum gas temperatures reached 100.7 ℃, 120 ℃, 235.5 ℃, 315.9 ℃, 379.9 ℃, and 418.8 ℃, respectively... Maxgas_temp = 2.9 × SOC +80.75 (R2 = 0.97827)

claims.claim: The proportion of CO2 in vent gas decreases with increasing SOC, from approximately 61% at 25% SOC to 40% at 50% SOC and 12% at 75% SOC, attributed to altered electrolyte decomposition pathways at higher lithium concentrations.
claims.quote: The proportion of CO2 decreases with increasing SOC (from 61 % to 40–12 %), a trend attributed to the alterations in the decomposition pathways of the electrolyte. At higher SOCs, the increased lithium concentration within the cell promotes reactions between the active materials and the electrolyte, resulting in a preference for the production of H2 or hydrocarbons.

claims.claim: The maximum surface temperature of NCM523 batteries during rapid penetration-induced thermal runaway under critical thermal load (100°C) increases with SOC, from 122.4°C at 0% SOC to 692.8°C at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: The specific data are as follows: 0 % SOC (122.4℃), 10 % SOC (123.4℃), 20 % SOC (124.9℃), 25 % SOC (146.3℃), 30 % SOC (380.5℃), 50 % SOC (439.5℃), 75 % SOC (569.7℃), 100 % SOC (692.8℃). The experimental data show that Tmax is positively correlated with SOC

claims.claim: The maximum temperature rise rate of NCM523 batteries during rapid penetration-induced thermal runaway at 100°C initial thermal load increases with SOC: 0%, 10%, and 20% SOC batteries reach 2.2°C/s, 2.39°C/s, and 2.51°C/s respectively; 25% SOC reaches 7.31°C/s; and 30%, 50%, 75%, and 100% SOC reach 33.8°C/s, 78.2°C/s, 85.1°C/s, and 93.5°C/s respectively.
claims.quote: the temperature rise rates of 0 %, 10 % and 20 % SOC batteries are 2.2℃/s, 2.39℃/s and 2.51℃/s respectively, the temperature rise rate of 25 % SOC batteries is 7.31℃/s, the temperature rise rates of 30 %, 50 %, 75 % and 100 % SOC batteries are 33.8℃/s, 78.2℃/s, 85.1℃/s and 93.5℃/s respectively

claims.claim: Under rapid penetration at critical thermal load (100°C), NCM523 batteries at 0–20% SOC exhibit mild TR (temperature rise rate 1–5°C/s), 25% SOC exhibits moderate TR (5–10°C/s), and 30–100% SOC exhibit severe TR (>10°C/s).
claims.quote: it can be determined that 0 % − 20 % SOC batteries are in a mild TR state, 25 % SOC battery is in a moderate TR, and 30 % − 100 % SOC batteries are all in a severe TR state. This result clearly shows that the SOC level is positively correlated with the severity of TR.

claims.claim: When SOC ≥ 30%, penetration-induced severe thermal runaway causes the voltage to drop instantaneously to 0 V, whereas mild-to-moderate TR batteries (0–25% SOC) show a gradual voltage decline followed by a transient recovery rather than immediate zero potential.
claims.quote: In batteries with high SOC, severe TR triggered by penetration will cause the voltage to drop to zero potential instantaneously. The experimental observation shows that when the SOC ≥ 30 %, the intense electrochemical reaction caused by the penetration process makes the internal energy storage of the battery fully released, and the voltage drops to 0 V.

claims.claim: Rapid penetration of NCM523 batteries under critical thermal load (100°C) significantly reduced the TR trigger temperature compared to overheating alone; under overheating, TR trigger temperatures were 169.1°C, 178.2°C, 185.9°C, and 194.6°C for 100%, 75%, 50%, and 30% SOC respectively.
claims.quote: The experimental results showed that the TR trigger temperatures of batteries with 100 %, 75 %, 50 % and 30 % SOC states under overheating condition were 169.1℃, 178.2℃, 185.9℃ and 194.6℃, respectively. These results indicate that rapid penetration significantly reduced the TR trigger temperature threshold of NCM523 battery.

claims.claim: With increasing SOC from 0% to 100%, the eruption behavior of severe TR NCM523 batteries progresses through four modes: smoke diffusion (30% SOC), transverse spark jet (50% SOC), longitudinal spark jet (75% SOC), and flame jet (100% SOC), with the spark jet area increasing by up to 41.44% between 50% and 100% SOC.
claims.quote: with the increase of SOC, the eruption behavior of severe TR batteries experienced smoke diffusion mode, transverse spark jet mode, longitudinal spark jet mode and flame jet mode, and the spark jet area also increased significantly, with the maximum area increased by 41.44 %

claims.claim: When aviation hydraulic oil immersion height increases from 0 to 75 mm, peak battery surface temperature drops 63.5%, CO generation decreases 98.7%, and EX (hydrocarbon) generation is entirely eliminated.
claims.quote: When immersion height increases from 0 to 75 mm, peak battery surface temperature drops 63.5 %, CO generation decreases 98.7 %, and EX (hydrocarbon) generation is entirely eliminated.

claims.claim: Under non-immersion conditions, the LIB underwent thermal runaway with a peak temperature rise rate of 14.6°C/s, while at 25, 45, and 75 mm immersion heights the peak temperature rise rates were only 0.7, 0.5, and 0.4°C/s respectively, and no thermal runaway occurred in any immersed group.
claims.quote: the peak temperature rise rate of the non-immersed group reached 14.6 ◦C/s, far above the critical threshold, indicating that TR occurred. In contrast, the 25, 45, and 75 mm immersion groups showed the peak temperature rise rates of only 0.7, 0.5, and 0.4 ◦C/s, respectively. None of the batteries experienced TR.

claims.claim: Under hydraulic oil immersion conditions, increasing SOC from 0 to 100% causes peak battery surface temperature to decrease 12.4%, CO generation to decrease 70.3%, while HF concentration unexpectedly increases from 0 to 0.6 ppm.
claims.quote: When state of charge increases from 0 to 100 %, peak battery surface temperature decreases 12.4 %, CO generation decreases 70.3 %, while HF concentration unexpectedly increases from 0 to 0.6 ppm.

claims.claim: Under partial immersion (25 mm and 45 mm), the FED remains above 1, indicating high asphyxiant hazard, whereas under full immersion (75 mm) the FED drops below 1, indicating low gas asphyxiation.
claims.quote: the partial immersion batteries still exhibit high asphyxiation, the FED remains above 1, which will pose serious safety concerns in the confined aircraft environment. Under full immersion, the FED drop below 1, which is a low gas asphyxiation.

claims.claim: HF released during LIB thermal abuse in hydraulic oil immersion environments is neutralized by alkaline additives (phosphate or amine-based inhibitors) in the aviation hydraulic oil, causing HF concentration to decline after peaking; as immersion height increases, the extent of neutralization increases, resulting in complete HF neutralization at 75 mm immersion.
claims.quote: when the safety valve openings, HF contacts the hydraulic oil and is neutralized by its alkaline additives (aviation hydraulic oil commonly contains phosphate or amine-based inhibitors) via Eq. (20), leading to a progressive decrease in HF concentration. As the immersion height increases, the extent of this neutralization reaction increases, resulting in greater HF reduction and, at 75 mm immersion, complete HF neutralization.

claims.claim: Under natural ventilation, the hazardous volume (≥25% LEL) increases from approximately 0.09 m³ for a single-cell venting event to over 8 m³ for 36 cells, with significantly prolonged duration.
claims.quote: under natural ventilation, the hazardous volume (≥25% LEL) increases from approximately 0.09 m3 for a single-cell venting event to over 8 m3 for 36 cells, with significantly prolonged duration

claims.claim: Buoyancy effects cause persistent high-concentration stratification near the cabin ceiling during thermal runaway gas dispersion.
claims.quote: Buoyancy effects cause persistent high-concentration stratification near the cabin ceiling

claims.claim: Thermal runaway of battery packs located at corners or adjacent to walls tends to induce localized gas accumulation.
claims.quote: thermal runaway of packs located at corners or adjacent to walls tends to induce localized accumulation

claims.claim: Ventilation layout II at 1.5 m/s achieves a similar level of hazardous zone control as the conventional layout I at 2.5 m/s, with an additional peak concentration reduction of approximately 0.27% and all monitoring points registering peak concentrations below 2.5%.
claims.quote: the optimized layout at 1.5 m⋅s−1 achieving a similar level of control as the conventional layout at 2.5 m⋅s−1

claims.claim: For the 7_u scenario (corner battery pack position), the hazardous volume peaks at 12.17 m³ with the longest duration of 838 s, whereas the 9_u scenario peaks at only 1.24 m³ with a duration shorter than 300 s.
claims.quote: the 7_u scenario has the largest hazardous volume (12.17 m3) with the longest duration (838 s), indicating severe and persistent risks. In contrast, the hazardous volumes in the 7_m and 9_u scenarios are significantly smaller, with peak values of only 2.11 m3 and 1.24 m3, respectively, and durations shorter than 300 s

claims.claim: Overcharging causes a decrease in the Lower Explosion Limit (LEL) of thermal runaway gas of Li-ion batteries, resulting in increased danger.
claims.quote: Overcharging causes a decrease in the LEL of thermal runaway gas, and there is a significant difference in the LEL variation of thermal runaway gas under different charge and discharge temperature environments (CTE).

claims.claim: At a charge/discharge ambient temperature of 40°C, overcharging decreases the LEL of thermal runaway gas by 0.32%, which is greater than the 0.19% decrease observed at 30°C.
claims.quote: In the charge/discharge ambient temperature of 30°C, the LEL decreases by 0.19% with the increase of the cutoff voltage, while in the charge/discharge environment of 40°C, the overcharge decreases the LEL by 0.32%.

claims.claim: The LEL of thermal runaway gas is lowest for batteries charged and discharged at 40°C, and in the range of 40–60°C, LEL increases with increasing temperature.
claims.quote: In the charge/discharge ambient temperature of 40–60°C, the LEL increases with the increase of temperature, and the change of LEL is relatively small and the pattern is not obvious at 50–60°C. The LEL of the battery charged and discharged in the ambient temperature of 40°C is the lowest.

claims.claim: The decrease in LEL of thermal runaway gas mixture is caused by a decrease in CO2 content and an increase in vaporized DMC content in the gas.
claims.quote: The decrease of thermal runaway gas LEL is due to the decrease of CO2 content in the gas and the increase of vaporized Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DMC) content.

claims.claim: At 40°C CTE, the percentage of gaseous DMC among thermal runaway gases is 4% higher than at 30°C for normally charged batteries (4.2 V), and 20% higher at a cutoff voltage of 4.4 V.
claims.quote: the percentage of gaseous DMC among the thermal runaway gases of the Li-ion battery at 40°C CTE is 4% higher than that at 30°C, as shown in Fig. 4 (4.2 V). The gas increment is more pronounced under overcharge conditions, with 20% higher at a cutoff voltage of 4.4 V.

claims.claim: The exothermic enthalpy (ΔH) of the anode material mixture reaction decreases with increasing cut-off voltage: 25.35 J/g at 4.2 V, 15.13 J/g at 4.3 V, and 10.11 J/g at 4.4 V, indicating less electrolyte reacts with anode material as overcharge degree increases.
claims.quote: The ΔH of exotherm of the Li-ion battery anode material mixture with a cutoff voltage of 4.2 V is 25.34802 J/g, ΔH of 4.3 V is 15.12939 J/g, and ΔH of 4.4 V is 10.11289 J/g. The amount of exotherm decreases with increasing cutoff voltage, indicating that less electrolyte reacts with the anode material as the degree of overcharge increases.

claims.claim: The overcharge cycle leads to thickening of the SEI membrane, causing incomplete reaction to produce CO2 during thermal runaway, resulting in decreased CO2 content and increased CO content in the gas mixture.
claims.quote: The overcharge cycle leads to the thickening of the SEI membrane, and the heat does not react completely to produce CO2 in the thermal runaway stage. The decrease in CO2 content and the increase in CO content may come from the incomplete reaction of sub-stable substances and the reaction between electrolyte and lithium salt.

claims.claim: The LEL of CO is 12%, which is significantly higher than that of other combustible hydrocarbons (1.5%–5%).
claims.quote: the LEL of CO is 12%, which is significantly higher than that of other combustible hydrocarbons (1.5%–5%)

claims.claim: At temperatures above 50°C CTE, the content of vaporized DMC in thermal runaway gas decreases compared to 40°C, because high-temperature improves cathode material activity and promotes earlier reaction between DMC and the negative electrode material.
claims.quote: increasing the CTE can improve the activity of the materials inside the Li-ion battery and inhibit lithium precipitation, which may promote the reaction between the cathode material and DMC, thus reducing the content of vaporized DMC in the thermal runaway gas... the content of DMC in the thermal runaway gas of the battery at 50°C as well as 60°C CTE is lower than that at 40°C, indicating that the effect of cathode material activity on DMC is higher than that of internal gas production when the temperature is above 50°C.

claims.claim: The negative electrode material of a lithium battery in normal use (4.2 V) only undergoes melting reaction at 700°C or above, but overcharging to 4.4 V causes an additional weight loss step at 96°C and 665°C, indicating decreased thermal stability of the negative electrode material.
claims.quote: the negative electrode material of the lithium battery in normal use only undergoes melting reaction at 700°C or above, and overcharging decreases the thermal stability of the negative electrode material... the negative electrode materials of Li-ion batteries with cutoff voltages of 4.4 V experience two weight loss steps at 96°C and 665°C.

claims.claim: The onset temperature of thermal runaway (T_onset) monotonically increases from 353 K to 417 K as SOC decreases from 120% to 30% for NCM cells.
claims.quote: T_onset monotonically increases from 353 K to 417 K with SOC decreasing from 120% to 30%

claims.claim: The maximum temperature (T_max) during thermal runaway decreases with decreasing SOC for NCM cells, indicating less reactive exothermic reactions at lower SOC.
claims.quote: T_max is decreased with decreased SOC, indicating the corresponding cell is less reactive with suppressed exothermic reactions

claims.claim: For NCM cells with SOC of 120%, the cell temperature rapidly increases from approximately 450 K to approximately 700 K with a maximum temperature rise rate (dT/dt)_max of order 10^4 K/min during Stage III.
claims.quote: Taking the SOC 120% cell as an example, the cell temperature rapidly increases from ∼ 450 K to ∼ 700 K with (𝑑𝑇 ∕𝑑𝑡)𝑚𝑎𝑥 of (104) K⋅min−1

claims.claim: For NCM cells with SOC of 30%, the maximum temperature rise rate (dT/dt)_max decreases to order 10^2 K/min with T_max of approximately 600 K during Stage III.
claims.quote: for the SOC 30% cell, (𝑑𝑇 ∕𝑑𝑡)𝑚𝑎𝑥 decreases to (102) K⋅min−1 with 𝑇𝑚𝑎𝑥 of ∼ 600 K

claims.claim: As SOC increases from 50% to 120%, the total mass loss of NCM cells after thermal runaway increases by approximately a factor of two.
claims.quote: As SOC increases from 50% to 120%, the total mass loss increases by approximately a factor of two, indicating a more complete reactive material decomposition within the cell

claims.claim: The NCM811 prismatic cell (62 Ah) discharged 4.83 mol of combustible gas during thermal runaway, with gas generation rate of 0.0779 mol/Ah.
claims.quote: By calculating the difference in gas volume before and after the experiment, the amount of gas is determined to be 4.83 mol. The gas generation of this battery is 0.0779 mol/Ah.

claims.claim: The main venting gas components of the NCM811 battery during thermal runaway are CO2 (28.6%), CO (27.9%), H2 (20.5%), C2H4 (12.5%), and CH4 (5.5%), with approximately 68.1% of the venting gas being flammable.
claims.quote: the main components of the venting gas of NCM811 battery in the order of high to low are: CO2, CO, H2, C2H4, CH4, O2. About 68.1 % of the venting gas is flammable, which has potential combustion risk.

claims.claim: The area 1 m above the safety valve exhibits the highest combustion risk and greatest explosive power for flammable gas vented from the NCM811 battery.
claims.quote: The high-risk area is 1 m above the safety valve. Experimental results indicate that explosions occur in locations distant from the safety valve. At 5.97 s and 8.58 s, fireballs appeared near the location 1 m above the safety valve.

claims.claim: Near the safety valve outlet, the combustible gas concentration exceeds the upper explosive limit (UEL), resulting in a low combustion risk index of zero at that location; the highest combustion risk is located farther from the valve.
claims.quote: At the battery safety valve, the flammable gas concentration exceeds the UEL with a risk index of 0. Differential diffusion of gas concentrations on either side of the safety valve creates an ignition risk zone. Far from the safety valve, the flammable gas concentration falls within the explosive range, forming a large risk area.

claims.claim: As jet velocity of the battery decreases, the risk of combustible gas explosion and combustion at the safety valve opening increases, with the highest risk period occurring during the initial and final stages of the jet.
claims.quote: As the jet velocity of the battery decreases the risk of combustible gas explosion and combustion at the safety valve opening increases. The period with the highest risk of jet-induced explosion and combustion occurs during the initial and final stages of the jet.

claims.claim: The SEI layer breaks down at a temperature between 80 and 120°C, releasing reducing gases including H2 and C2H4.
claims.quote: SEI layer breaks down at a temperature between 80 and 120 ◦C in the battery, releasing reducing gases including H2 and C2H4.

claims.claim: For NCM batteries, when the internal temperature exceeds 220°C, the redox reaction between cathode and anode occurs, which can instantly raise the internal temperature to above 900°C.
claims.quote: For Li(NixCoyMn1-x-y)O2 (NCM) battery, when the internal temperature of the battery exceeds 220 ◦C, the redox reaction will occur between the cathode and the anode material, and the reaction will be carried out simultaneously with a large number of side reactions, which can instantly raise the internal temperature of the battery to >900 ◦C.

claims.claim: Reducing gas generated at the anode-electrolyte interface migrates to the cathode, reacts with cathode material, causes early degradation, releases heat, and advances the decomposition reaction temperature of the cathode.
claims.quote: the reducing gas generated at the anode - electrolyte interface freely migrates to the highly oxidized cathode, which then reacts with the cathode material, causing an early degradation of the cathode material and leading to a large amount of heat release. Wang et al. [51] revealed the effect of reducing gas attack in the early stages of TR on heat accumulation and TR triggering, and found that reducing gas can further advance the decomposition reaction temperature of the cathode.

claims.claim: The solid-liquid phase change reaction of the cathode material starts at 140°C, generating CO2 and releasing O2 in a three-phase solid-liquid-gas mixture reaction.
claims.quote: the solid-liquid phase change reaction of the cathode material starts at a temperature of 140 ◦C. This process was tested to the generation of CO2, which is involved in the reaction between the cathode and the electrolyte, at which time it is a three-phase solid-liquid-gas mixture reaction and releases O2.

claims.claim: CO2, CO, H2, C2H4, CH4, and C2H6 make up more than 90% of the total vent gases from LIB thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Among them, CO2, CO, H2, C2H4, CH4 and C2H6 make up the majority of the gases that are released, accounting for >90 % of the total vent gases.

claims.claim: At 0.2 MPa external pressure, thermal runaway propagation speed increases by approximately 65% compared to 0 MPa.
claims.quote: external pressure accelerates thermal runaway propagation (TRP), with propagation speed at 0.2 MPa increasing by approximately 65 % compared to 0 MPa.

claims.claim: Total gas yield decreases by approximately 28% at 0.1 MPa and 53% at 0.2 MPa relative to 0 MPa external pressure.
claims.quote: gas evolution analysis reveals a substantial reduction in total gas yield with increasing external pressure, exhibiting decreases of about 28 % at 0.1 MPa and 53 % at 0.2 MPa relative to 0 MPa.

claims.claim: External pressures of 0.1 and 0.2 MPa enhance interfacial contact within the battery, thereby increasing internal thermal conductivity and resulting in a more uniform temperature distribution, which raises the TR initiation temperature.
claims.quote: external pressures of 0.1 and 0.2 MPa enhance interfacial contact within the battery, thereby increasing internal thermal conductivity. This improvement results in a more uniform temperature distribution, which raises the TR initiation temperature

claims.claim: Increasing external pressure shifts the initial TR location inward from the battery edge toward the center.
claims.quote: shifts the initial TR location inward from the battery edge toward the center

claims.claim: The reduction in total gas yield with increasing external pressure is primarily attributed to earlier safety venting and prolonged electrolyte evaporation periods.
claims.quote: This reduction is primarily attributed to earlier safety venting and prolonged electrolyte evaporation periods.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, thermal runaway was observed during nail penetration, attributed to the occurrence of a secondary ISC, whereas batteries tested at lower SOC levels (0% and 50% SOC) did not show fire or explosion under similar experimental conditions.
claims.quote: At 100% SOC, thermal runaway was observed, attributed to the occurrence of a secondary ISC. By contrast, batteries tested at lower SOC levels (0% and 50% SOC) did not show fire or explosion, despite being subjected to similar experimental conditions.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway propagation in LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1-based batteries is eightfold faster than in LiFePO4-based batteries.
claims.quote: cathode chemistry (thermal runaway propagation of LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1‐based batteries is eightfold faster than that of LiFePO4‐based batteries)

claims.claim: When temperatures exceeded 233°C, all batteries initiated thermal runaway, triggered by significant shrinkage of the separator and reactions related to the cathode.
claims.quote: When temperatures exceeded 233°C, all batteries initiated thermal runaway, triggered by significant shrinkage of the separator and reactions related to the cathode.

claims.claim: Under high SOC conditions greater than 75% SOC, nails with higher conductivity enhanced internal Joule heat release, leading to more intense thermal runaway behavior, whereas under low SOC conditions less than 50% SOC, nails with higher conductivity helped dissipate heat, weakening the intensity of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Under high SOC conditions (greater than 75% SOC), nails with higher conductivity enhanced the internal Joule heat release of the battery, leading to more intense thermal runaway behavior. In contrast, under low SOC conditions (less than 50% SOC), nails with higher conductivity helped dissipate heat, thus weakening the intensity of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: At 90% SOC during nail penetration simulation, the battery temperature could reach up to 800°C with a voltage drop of 1.9 V.
claims.quote: At 90% SOC, the temperature could reach up to 800°C, with a voltage drop of 1.9 V.

claims.claim: Ceramic nails were more likely to trigger higher-temperature thermal runaway flames and increased fire risk compared to steel nails, and increased penetration depth and diameter led to greater mass loss with ceramic nails causing more damage than steel nails.
claims.quote: Ceramic nails were more likely to trigger higher‐temperature thermal runaway flames, increasing the fire risk compared to steel nails. Increased penetration depth and diameter led to greater mass loss, with ceramic nails causing more damage than steel nails.

claims.claim: The contact resistance for a nail in contact with pure active material film is approximately three orders of magnitude greater than that of the nail-to-bare-metal-foil configuration.
claims.quote: the combination of a nail with a pure active material film yields the highest contact resistance, which is approximately three orders of magnitude greater than that of the nail/bare metal foil setup.

claims.claim: Increasing nail penetration speed leads to a shorter time to thermal runaway and a higher peak temperature, and temperature nonuniformity within the battery intensifies at higher penetration speeds.
claims.quote: an increase in penetration speed leads to a shorter time to thermal runaway and a higher peak temperature. Mao et al. [33] observed that as the nail penetration speed increases, temperature nonuniformity within the battery intensifies, indicating that high‐speed penetration causes more severe damage.

claims.claim: Cells at 100% SOC retained approximately 53.7% of their initial mass after nail penetration thermal runaway, compared to 96.7% at 20% SOC, indicating that cells at higher SOC expel more battery components during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: cells retained approximately 53.7% of their initial mass at 100% SOC, as opposed to 96.7% at 20% SOC, indicating that cells at higher SOC expel more battery components during thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Solid-state architectures showed 0% thermal runaway incidence in nail penetration tests.
claims.quote: solid‐state architectures showing 0% thermal runaway incidence in nail penetration tests

claims.claim: The SEI membrane begins to decompose at 80–120 °C, releasing active lithium for an oxidative reaction with the electrolyte.
claims.quote: When the temperature reaches 80–120 °C, the SEI membrane on the negative electrode begins to decompose, releasing active lithium for an oxidative reaction with the electrolyte.

claims.claim: At 150–200 °C, the positive electrode material releases oxygen, which reacts violently with the electrolyte, significantly increasing the rate of heat production.
claims.quote: As the temperature further increases to 150–200 °C, the positive electrode material releases oxygen, which reacts violently with the electrolyte, significantly increasing the rate of heat production.

claims.claim: Once the temperature exceeds 200 °C, the electrolyte decomposes and the polyolefin diaphragm melts and shrinks, causing direct contact between the positive and negative electrodes.
claims.quote: Once the temperature exceeds 200 °C, the electrolyte decomposes, and the polyolefin diaphragm melts and shrinks, causing direct contact between the positive and negative electrodes.

claims.claim: The melting points of PP and PE separators are around 165 °C and 130 °C respectively, while a PP/PE/PP composite separator melts at around 170 °C and a ceramic-coated separator at around 200 °C.
claims.quote: The melting points of commonly used PP and PE separators are around 165 °C and 130 °C, respectively, while the melting point of a PP/PE/PP composite separator is around 170 °C, and that of a separator with a ceramic coating is around 200 °C.

claims.claim: When the SOC exceeded 60% in three-point bending tests on cylindrical LIBs, the battery experienced significant thermal runaway events such as explosions and fire.
claims.quote: Their results showed that when the SOC exceeded 60%, the battery experienced significant thermal runaway events, such as explosions and fire.

claims.claim: When temperature exceeds 260 °C, fluorinated binders react with intercalated lithium in the negative electrode to produce H2.
claims.quote: When the temperature exceeds 260 °C, fluorinated binders react with the intercalated lithium in the negative electrode to produce H2.

claims.claim: Under overcharging conditions, lithium dendrite deposition occurs on the negative electrode and can penetrate the diaphragm, while excessive lithium delithiation on the positive electrode causes structural collapse and increased oxygen release.
claims.quote: Under overcharging conditions, lithium dendrite deposition occurs on the negative electrode, penetrating the diaphragm, while excessive lithium delithiation on the positive electrode causes structural collapse and increased oxygen release.

claims.claim: Thermal insulation materials can significantly delay the propagation speed of thermal runaway within battery modules.
claims.quote: Hu et al. experimentally examined the effect of thermal insulation materials on thermal runaway propagation and found that such materials can significantly delay the propagation speed.

claims.claim: In a compression test of commercial ternary/graphite Li-ion batteries, indentation enhanced cycling performance when compressed from 4 mm to 6.8 mm over 620 cycles, but the effect became detrimental when indentation depth exceeded 6.5 mm.
claims.quote: The test cells were compressed from 4 mm to 6.8 mm and cycled 620 times. The results indicated that indentation enhanced the cycling performance of the test cells, although the effect became detrimental when the indentation depth exceeded 6.5 mm.

claims.claim: A 1 mm thick phase change material effectively reduced battery temperature under high discharge conditions.
claims.quote: MoraliU demonstrated that the thickness of the phase change material significantly affects the thermal management system's performance, with a 1 mm thick material effectively reducing battery temperature under high discharge conditions.

claims.claim: At a discharge rate of 25C, thermal runaway occurred and the maximum temperature exceeded 500°C.
claims.quote: Especially in the 25C rate condition, the maximum temperature increased exceeds 500 ◦C.

claims.claim: Under the same C-rate conditions, batteries with lower SOC exhibited a faster surface temperature rise rate, while batteries with higher SOC reached a higher maximum surface temperature.
claims.quote: The surface temperature rise rate of batteries with low SOC were faster, and the maximum surface temperature rise of batteries with high SOC were higher.

claims.claim: At 20C discharge rate, the 50% SOC battery (battery 3) had average temperature rise rates of 2.21°C/s, 2.70°C/s, and 4.42°C/s at T1, T2, and T3 respectively, with maximum temperature rises of 100.17°C, 118.40°C, and 128.99°C.
claims.quote: the average temperature rise rate of battery 3 with the state of charge (SOC) of 50 % at T1, T2, and T3 were 2.21◦C/s, 2.70◦C/s, and 4.42◦C/s, respectively, and the maximum temperature rise were 100.17◦C, 118.40◦C, and 128.99◦C respectively.

claims.claim: At 20C discharge rate, the 100% SOC battery (battery 8) had lower average temperature rise rates (1.35°C/s, 1.46°C/s, 3.03°C/s) but higher maximum temperature rise at T3 (156.50°C) compared to the 50% SOC battery at the same rate.
claims.quote: the average temperature rise rate of battery 8 with a state of charge of 100 % at T1, T2, and T3 were 1.35 ◦C/s, 1.46 ◦C/s, and 3.03 ◦C/s, respectively, and the maximum temperature rise were 91.96◦C, 110.08◦C, 156.50◦C.

claims.claim: In the same C-rate and SOC conditions, the negative electrode (T3) had the highest temperature during short circuit, followed by the center (T2), and the positive electrode (T1) had the lowest temperature.
claims.quote: It indicated that the negative electrode of the battery has the highest temperature during the short-circuit process, followed by the center temperature and finally the positive electrode temperature.

claims.claim: Separator shrinkage and tearing were identified as the main causes of jelly roll deformation in batteries subjected to external short circuit.
claims.quote: It is concluded that shrinkage and tearing of the separator were the main causes of the deformation of the battery jelly roll.

claims.claim: When the separator temperature reached 130°C, the micropores of the separator closed and irreversibly blocked, and at 150°C the separator appeared visibly wrinkled and destroyed completely.
claims.quote: when the temperature increased to 130◦C, the internal temperature reached the melting point of the polymer and the micropores of the separator closed and irreversibly blocked the pores of the separator. When the temperature was further increased to 150◦C, the separator appeared to be visibly wrinkled and destroyed completely.

claims.claim: During external short circuit at 100% SOC, the voltage curve showed a rapid initial drop followed by a distinct voltage platform near 2.75 V, caused by the high temperature triggering separator pore closure which hindered Li+ transport.
claims.quote: The presence of a distinct voltage platform in the high SOC group was caused by the high temperature during the external short circuit of the battery that triggered the separator to close its holes, thus hindered the transport of Li+.

claims.claim: Discharge capacity of short-circuit batteries decreased linearly with increasing short-circuit current, with the 100% SOC battery discharge capacity being slightly higher than the 50% SOC battery.
claims.quote: the discharge capacity of the 100 % SOC short-circuit battery was slightly higher than that of the 50 % SOC short-circuit battery, which decreased linearly with the increase of the short-circuit currents.

claims.claim: Over-discharge during the short circuit process caused copper from the negative electrode current collector to oxidize, migrate across the separator, and deposit as Cu metal on the positive electrode surface.
claims.quote: the negative electrode current collector copper foil was oxidized to Cu+ and Cu2+ at high voltage. At the same time, the positive voltage decreased, and these copper ions would be migrated from the negative electrode across the separator to the surface of the positive electrode under the action of the electro-potential difference, and be reduced to Cu+ and Cu metal at the positive electrode, which formed the Cu deposition.

claims.claim: At the triggering temperature T2, the internal temperature of the NCA||Gr battery was 438.3°C while the surface temperature measured 179.9°C, a difference of 258.4°C.
claims.quote: The T2 value corresponding to the surface temperature is 179.9°C, at the same time, the internal temperature has reached 438.3°C, with a difference of 258.4°C.

claims.claim: Using only surface temperature measurement causes incorrect attribution of internal short circuit timing, leading to the false conclusion that internal short circuit occurs after T2 and does not contribute to thermal runaway triggering.
claims.quote: If only the surface temperature is considered in the analysis of thermal runaway behavior, it will be wrongly believed that the internal short circuit occurs after T2 and does not contribute to the thermal runaway triggering, which directly affects the understanding of thermal runaway mechanism and the judgment of whether the internal short circuit is used as the input heat source of the model.

claims.claim: In the self-heating stage (T1 to T2) of NCA||Gr battery thermal runaway, the An+Ele system contributes 53.8% of heat, internal short circuit contributes 27.5%, and the Ca+An system contributes 18.7%.
claims.quote: An+Ele system contributes 53.8% of the heat for thermal runaway triggering, internal short circuit 27.5%, and Ca + An system 18.7%.

claims.claim: The heat generated by internal short circuit is released instantaneously and directly triggers thermal runaway, causing the battery temperature rate to reach 1°C/s.
claims.quote: The heat generated by the internal short circuit is released instantly, making the battery temperature rate reach 1°C s−1. After the thermal runaway is triggered, the reaction between decomposition products of LiPF6 and anode, the reaction of electrolyte solvent with intercalated lithium and the reaction of electrolyte solvent with cathode oxygen release all contribute more heat to make the battery temperature reach the maximum.

claims.claim: After thermal runaway is triggered (T2 to T3), the An+Ele system contributes 59.1%, the Ca+An system contributes 35.6%, and internal short circuit contributes only 5.4% to the temperature rise.
claims.quote: After thermal runaway triggering(T2 ~ T3), QCEI is not contributing, the descending order of heat contribution is: QAn+E > QO2+An > QLi+ES > QCa+B > Qec. An+Ele system contributes 59.1%, internal short circuit 5.4% and Ca + An system 35.6% to the temperature rise after thermal runaway triggering.

claims.claim: SEI membrane decomposition is considered the first step in thermal runaway and is an exothermic process whose released heat accumulates inside the battery, accelerating thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The decomposition of SEI membranes is widely considered to be the first step in thermal runaway. SEI membrane decomposition is an exothermic process, and the heat released from decomposition will accumulate inside the battery, accelerating the occurrence of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Microcalorimetry experiments showed two exothermic peaks during SEI decomposition occurring at approximately 45 degrees Celsius and 100 degrees Celsius.
claims.quote: The results of the microcalorimetry experiment show that there are two exothermic peaks. These peaks occur at temperatures approximately around 45 degrees centigrade and 100 degrees centigrade.

claims.claim: At 105 degrees Celsius, the SEI film appears dispersed and incomplete, indicating that high temperatures cause the SEI film to undergo decomposition leading to a looser structure.
claims.quote: after treatment at temperature of 105 degrees centigrade, the SEI film appears dispersed and incomplete. This indicates that at high temperatures, the SEI film undergoes decomposition to some extent, leading to a looser structure.

claims.claim: The SEI impedance value dropped by more than 70% when the temperature reached 75 degrees Celsius compared to the room temperature reference value, making SEI impedance more sensitive than battery terminal voltage to temperature changes.
claims.quote: dropped more than 70%. Through the analysis, it can be seen that the SEI impedance is more sensitive than the battery voltage when temperature changes. Thereafter, comparing with the battery terminal voltage, it is better to use the SEI impedance as a proper sign for the thermal runaway risk of the battery.

claims.claim: The impedance value of the SEI component (P2) almost disappeared when the holding time exceeded 20 minutes at 105 degrees Celsius, indicating severe damage or complete disappearance of the SEI film.
claims.quote: The impedance value of P2 almost disappeared when the holding time exceeded 20 minutes when the cell is hold at 105 degrees Celsius. At this time, the structure of SEI film may be seriously damaged or completely disappeared.

claims.claim: The exothermic decomposition of the SEI around 100–150°C has been reported to initiate thermal runaway in lithium ion batteries.
claims.quote: The exothermic decomposition of the SEI around 100~150oC has been reported to initiate thermal runaway in LIBs.

claims.claim: LEDC thermally decomposes over the temperature range of 50–300°C to evolve CO2 and C2H4 gases, leaving lithium propionate (CH3CH2CO2Li) and Li2CO3 as solid residues.
claims.quote: Over the temperature range of 50~300oC, LEDC decomposes to evolve CO2 and C2H4 gasses leaving lithium propionate (CH3CH2CO2Li) and Li2CO3 as solid residues.

claims.claim: Lithium propionate decomposes over the temperature range of 300–600°C to evolve pentanone, leaving Li2CO3 as a residual solid.
claims.quote: The lithium propionate decomposes over the temperature range of 300~600oC to evolve pentanone leaving Li2CO3 as a residual solid.

claims.claim: Li2CO3 decomposes above 600°C to evolve CO2, leaving Li2O as a residual solid.
claims.quote: Finally, the Li2CO3 decomposes over 600oC to evolve CO2 leaving Li2O as a residual solid.

claims.claim: TGA measurements of Si electrodes after various numbers of cycles suggest that LEDC on Si electrodes thermally decomposes during cycling to form lithium propionate and Li2CO3, resulting in increased complexity of the SEI.
claims.quote: TGA measurements of Si electrodes after various numbers of cycles suggest that the LEDC on Si electrodes thermally decomposes during cycling to form lithium propionate and Li2CO3, resulting in increased complexity of the SEI.

claims.claim: The Li2CO3 decomposition in the SEI on cycled Si electrodes begins at a lower temperature than that for independently prepared LEDC, which is attributed to the high content of carbon in the Si electrode causing carbothermal reduction.
claims.quote: The Li2CO3 decomposition in the SEI begins at a lower temperature than that for the independently prepared LEDC (Figure 8a). This can be attributed to high content of carbon in the Si electrode. Carbon has been known to lead to carbothermal reduction, altering the decomposition kinetics and products, while thermal decomposition of pure Li2CO3 is known to begin above 800oC.

claims.claim: The weight loss in the 250–350°C range in cycled Si electrodes results from the thermal decomposition of LixPOyFz, a decomposition product of LiPF6 reported as a component of the SEI.
claims.quote: the thermal decomposition of LixPOyFz, a decomposition product LiPF6 which has frequently been reported as a component of the SEI, has been investigated. Independently obtained or prepared lithium phosphate derivatives undergo thermal decomposition around 300oC... the weight loss in the 250~350oC range results from the thermal decomposition of LixPOyFz.

claims.claim: The peaks characteristic of RCO2Li and Li2CO3 show greater increases in intensity between the 30th and 50th cycles than that observed for LEDC, suggesting that RCO2Li and Li2CO3 were present in the SEI prior to TGA measurement, consistent with LEDC thermally decomposing during prolonged cycling.
claims.quote: the intensity ratios between LEDC, RCO2Li, and Li2CO3 change as a function of cycling. In particular, the peaks characteristic of RCO2Li and Li2CO3 have greater increases in intensity between the 30th and 50th cycles than that observed for LEDC. This suggests that not all the RCO2Li and Li2CO3 are products of LEDC decomposition during the TGA experiment. Rather, the RCO2Li and Li2CO3 were present in the SEI prior to the TGA measurement. This is consistent with LEDC thermally decomposing during prolonged cycling to generate RCO2Li, and Li2CO3 in the SEI.

claims.claim: Ethylene (C2H4) evolution from LEDC clearly begins at approximately 90°C before CO2 evolution, suggesting that an intermediate, lithium peroxodicarbonate (Li2C2O6), is generated.
claims.quote: it is interesting that ethylene evolution clearly begins at ~90oC (2 min at 20oC min-1) before CO2 evolution suggesting that an intermediate, lithium peroxodicarbonate (Li2C2O6), is generated.

claims.claim: The thermal decomposition of LEDC could be accelerated by the surface of the lithiated silicon electrode or acidic decomposition products of the LiPF6 electrolyte.
claims.quote: The LEDC decomposition could be accelerated by the surface of the lithiated silicon electrode or acidic decomposition products of the LiPF6 electrolyte.

claims.claim: The ratio of triggering energy of self-heat onset to total self-heat generation remained consistent across LFP batteries with a mean value of 24.5%, indicating that LFP batteries obtain approximately 80% of their energy from internal exothermic reactions during adiabatic thermal abuse.
claims.quote: The mean value of the ratio was 24.5%, indicating that lithium iron phosphate batteries obtain most of the energy (generally 80%) from internal exothermic reactions during adiabatic thermal abuse.

claims.claim: The triggering energy of thermal runaway remained constant at approximately 670 kJ when various heating powers (300–600 W) were applied to a 280 Ah LFP battery, with an inaccuracy of only 2.5%, accounting for 20.8% of the theoretical energy stored inside.
claims.quote: TE of thermal runaway remains constant when multiple heating powers are used to activate thermal runaway and the inaccuracy of triggering energy is only 2.5%... For the 280 Ah LFP battery investigated in this paper, 670 kJ is the minimum triggering energy of thermal runaway, accounting for 20.8% theoretical energy stored inside.

claims.claim: Approximately 65.5% of the self-heat generation in LFP batteries is released during thermal runaway in a short period of time, causing a steep rise in temperature.
claims.quote: This means almost 65.5% of the self-heat will be released in a short time, leading to a steep rise in the temperature.

claims.claim: Increasing the number of cells (thinner cells) inside a battery accelerates thermal runaway propagation by increasing the average temperature of each thinner cell, with cell temperature and heat generation having positive feedback.
claims.quote: the thinner the cells, the faster thermal runaway propagates... more cells and thinner cells can accelerate the thermal runaway propagation by increasing the average temperature of the thinner cells. This serves as a reminder to manufacturers that to offer safer performance in multi-cell setups, an additional thermal-isolation layer must be placed between two cells.

claims.claim: Battery thickness has little impact on maximum temperature but causes thermal runaway triggering time and time to maximum temperature to grow linearly with battery thickness.
claims.quote: the battery thickness has little impact on the maximum temperature but changes the thermal runaway process in terms of triggering time for thermal runaway Tonset. The triggering time for thermal runaway and the time required to achieve the maximum temperature grow linearly with battery thickness.

claims.claim: The ratio of triggering energy of self-heat onset to total self-heat generation remained consistent across LFP batteries in an adiabatic environment, with a mean value of 24.5% and a median of 23.1%.
claims.quote: Although a wide variance of onset temperature and maximum temperature exists among LFP batteries, consistency exists in the ratio of TE of onset to total heat generation shows good consistency and stability among the references. Fig. 6 provides a quick overview of the battery TR features. Based on the statistics of characteristic and typical temperature Tonset and Tmax in the references, the ratio of TE of onset to total self-generation heat is consistent with the range of 15%–52%. Although a wide variance of onset temperature and maximum temperature exists among LFP batteries, consistency exists in the ratio of TE of onset to total self-generation heat. The mean value is 24.5%, and the median is 23.1%.

claims.claim: Under adiabatic conditions, LFP batteries obtain approximately 80% of their energy from internal exothermic reactions, with external input energy accounting for approximately 20%.
claims.quote: Under the adiabatic condition, LFP battery gets most of the energy sources from internal exothermic reactions, which generally respond for 80%, and the input energy accounts for approximately 20%.

claims.claim: For the 280 Ah LFP battery investigated, the triggering energy of thermal runaway remained constant at approximately 670 kJ (about 20.8% of theoretical stored energy) regardless of which heating power was applied, with an inaccuracy of only 2.5%.
claims.quote: TE of thermal runaway remains constant when multiple heating powers are used to activate thermal runaway and the inaccuracy of triggering energy is only 2.5%... For the 280 Ah LFP battery investigated in this paper, 670 kJ is the minimum triggering energy of thermal runaway, accounting for 20.8% theoretical energy stored inside.

claims.claim: Approximately 65.5% of the total self-heat generation in LFP batteries is released during thermal runaway in a short period of time.
claims.quote: This means almost 65.5% of the self-heat will be released in a short time, leading to a steep rise in the temperature.

claims.claim: Increasing the number of cells (thinner cells) inside a battery accelerates thermal runaway propagation by raising the average temperature of each thinner cell, while the maximum temperature shows little variation.
claims.quote: the thinner the cells, the faster thermal runaway propagates... In terms of characteristic battery temperature, Tmax exhibits little variation... more cells and thinner cells can accelerate the thermal runaway propagation by increasing the average temperature of the thinner cells.

claims.claim: The sound-based early warning system issues alerts on average at 9352s, which is approximately 7000s earlier than temperature and pressure-based warning methods.
claims.quote: the sound-based thermal runaway warning can be achieved in 9352s. The average exceeds the temperature and sound-based warning by about 7000s.

claims.claim: Temperature and stress-based warnings are issued around the T2 moment, at which point the battery is approaching or entering a critical state, leaving insufficient response time.
claims.quote: both temperature- and stress-based warnings are issued around T2, indicating their relevance in thermal runaway monitoring. However, by the time these changes are detected, the battery system is approaching or entering a critical state, making it difficult for warnings based on such metrics to provide sufficient response time.

claims.claim: When the continuous sampling temperature change rate exceeds 1°C/s and lasts for 3s, the temperature at the middle sampling moment is taken as T2, the critical point from slow to fast warming and the trigger moment of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: T2 represents the critical point of the battery from slow to fast warming, which is the trigger moment of thermal runaway. When the continuous sampling temperature change rate exceeds 1 ◦C/s and lasts for 3s, the temperature at the middle sampling moment is taken as T2.

claims.claim: Under the 3.5C overcharge condition, a significant acoustic signal change at 11462.4s indicated an abnormality within the lithium-ion battery, occurring much earlier than thermal runaway onset.
claims.quote: Under the 3.5C overcharge condition, the significant change at 11462.4s indicates an abnormality within the Lithium-ion battery.

claims.claim: Adding white noise reduced the second-level thermal runaway warning accuracy from 98.08% to 95.68%, while the first-level warning results remained nearly identical to those without noise.
claims.quote: In the second-level thermal runaway warning, the introduction of white noise reduced the accuracy from 98.08 % to 95.68 %. Despite the noise interference, the system maintained a high level of recognition accuracy... A comparison revealed that the first-level thermal runaway warning results, after adding white noise, were nearly identical to those without it.

claims.claim: In the EV-ARC test, the onset of self-heating temperature T1 is defined as the internal temperature when dT/dt reaches 0.02 °C/min, and the triggering temperature T2 is defined as the internal temperature when dT/dt reaches 1 °C/s.
claims.quote: When dT/dt arrives at the threshold value, i.e., 0.02 °C/min, the temperature obtained from the implanted thermocouple is defined as the onset of self-heating temperature T1... The temperature of the implanted thermocouple at this moment is defined as the triggering temperature T2.

claims.claim: For the tested prismatic NMC/graphite cell, the characteristic temperatures from the internal thermocouple were T1 = 72.4 °C, T2 = 222.7 °C, and T3 = 994.8 °C, while the maximum surface temperature was only 565.9 °C.
claims.quote: the battery cell starts self-heating when its temperature exceeds T1 (72.4 °C), and it experiences a long while before it reaches the onset temperature T2 (222.7 °C) of TR. Once the TR is triggered, the internal temperature from the implanted thermocouple quickly goes up to the max value T3 (994.8 °C)... the max temperature from the surface thermocouple is only 565.9 °C, which is significantly lower than T3.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature variation rate from the internal thermocouple reached 531 °C/s shortly after T2, while the maximum from the surface thermocouple was only 49.2 °C/s, a difference of approximately one order of magnitude.
claims.quote: the temperature variation rate related to the central thermocouple accelerates once temperature exceeds T1, and it can go up to the maximum of 531 °C/s shortly after T2... the temperature variation rate related to the surface thermocouple is much less by one order of magnitude, and the maximum is only 49.2 °C/s.

claims.claim: In the battery pack model, the TR propagation time interval related to silicon rubber pads (approximately 120 s) was much greater than that between adjacent battery cells (approximately 60 s), and the interval related to silicon rubber pads decreased with the progress of TR propagation.
claims.quote: the TR propagation time interval related to a silicon rubber pad (around 120 s) is much greater than that between adjacent battery cells (around 60 s)... the time interval related to silicon rubber pads becomes shorter with the progress of TR propagation, and the gap between time intervals is as large as 11 s.

claims.claim: Under severe operating conditions (fast charging and uphill climbing), TR propagation time intervals were shorter than under normal working conditions, because battery cells generated heat prior to TR onset, raising their initial temperature to approximately 47.2 °C and 47.4 °C respectively, compared to 28.0 °C after rapid heating.
claims.quote: the TR propagation time intervals in the severe operating conditions are apparently shorter... the final temperatures of battery cells after these three severe working conditions are different, that is, 28.0 °C, 47.2 °C and 47.4 °C after the condition of rapid heating, fast charging and uphill climbing, respectively. From this comparison, it is confirmed that the initial temperature of battery cells has an important impact on TR propagation time intervals.

claims.claim: The removal of the SEI layer typically occurs at temperatures ranging from 69 to 120 °C, followed by exothermic reactions between the electrolyte and the graphite anode triggering around 100 °C to 130 °C.
claims.quote: The removal of the SEI layer typically occurs at temperatures ranging from 69 to 120 ◦C [2], followed by exothermic reactions between the electrolyte and the graphite anode triggering around 100 ◦C to 130 ◦C.

claims.claim: As temperature increases to 135–200 °C, the separator melts or shrinks, potentially leading to internal short circuits when the cathode and anode come into contact, and ISCs act as accelerators in the thermal runaway process.
claims.quote: As the temperature increases to 135–200 ◦C, the separator melts or shrinks, potentially leading to ISCs when the cathode and anode come into contact. ISCs act as accelerators in the thermal runaway process.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway can still occur even without internal short circuits if there is sufficient heat.
claims.quote: it is important to note that the thermal runaway process can still occur even without ISCs if there is sufficient heat

claims.claim: The cathode begins to decompose and react with the electrolyte at temperatures ranging from 100 to 500 °C depending on the cathode material, electrolyte decomposition occurs at temperatures greater than 200 °C, and the binder reacts with the cathode material at temperatures exceeding 260 °C.
claims.quote: the cathode begins to decompose and react with the electrolyte at temperatures ranging from 100 to 500 ◦C, depending on the cathode material. Following this, electrolyte decomposition occurs at temperatures greater than 200 ◦C, and finally the binder reacts with the cathode material at temperatures exceeding 260 ◦C.

claims.claim: Higher SOC levels led to increased mass loss in LCO cathodes, emphasising the SOC's impact on cathode thermal stability.
claims.quote: Veluchamy et al. [22] examined LCO cathodes at different SOC levels and found that higher SOC levels led to increased mass loss, emphasising the SOC's impact on cathode stability.

claims.claim: The circumferential strain-rate first exceeds 0.1 με/s at 302 s, while venting occurs at 413 s and thermal runaway at 443 s, corresponding to a lead time of over 100 s compared to conventional venting-based alerts and more than 140 s ahead of temperature-based confirmation.
claims.quote: It shows that the circumferential strain-rate first exceeds 0.1 με/s at 302 s, while venting occurs at 413 s and thermal runaway at 443 s. This corresponds to a lead time of over 100s compared to conventional venting-based alerts, and more than 140 s ahead of temperature-based confirmation.

claims.claim: The strain-rate threshold of 0.2 με/s is reached at 345 s, providing a practical early warning signal during rapid gas accumulation.
claims.quote: The strain-rate threshold of 0.2 με/s is reached at 345 s, providing a practical early warning signal during rapid gas accumulation.

claims.claim: Under fully constrained boundary conditions, the maximum strain levels are significantly lower and the strain growth occurs with greater delay compared to two-sided clamping, because full structural confinement restricts radial expansion and causes pressure-induced volumetric changes to be accommodated internally as compressive stress accumulation rather than external deformation.
claims.quote: under the fully constrained condition shown in Fig. 7(c) and (d), the maximum strain levels are significantly lower, and the strain growth occurs with greater delay. The strain rate curve in Fig. 7(d) exhibits a muted response, with a delayed peak and substantially reduced magnitude. These results suggest that full structural confinement markedly restricts radial expansion of the casing, causing the pressure-induced volumetric changes to be accommodated internally as compressive stress accumulation rather than external deformation.

claims.claim: The biaxial clamping configuration triggers the earliest warning response, with the initial strain-rate increase observed at tst = 283 s and the onset of critical expansion at tac = 327 s, whereas the free-expansion case shows tst = 302 s and tac = 310 s, and the fully constrained case shows tst = 345 s and tac = 337 s.
claims.quote: the biaxial clamping configuration triggers the earliest warning response, with the initial strain-rate increase observed at tst = 283 s and the onset of critical expansion at tac = 327s, yielding a total time of 610 s. In contrast, both the free-expansion and fully constrained cases exhibit similar response timings, with tst = 302 and tac = 310 s, and tst = 345 s and tac = 337 s, respectively

claims.claim: Near the geometric centre of the cylindrical cell, the strain magnitude reaches a peak of 13,000 με, but as the measurement point moves radially outward the strain rapidly attenuates and stabilizes beyond r > 4 mm, dropping below 2000 με under fully constrained conditions.
claims.quote: Near the geometric centre of the cylindrical cell, the strain magnitude reaches its peak to 13,000 με. However, as the measurement point moves radially outward, the strain rapidly attenuates and tends to stabilize beyond r > 4 mm, especially under fully constrained conditions, where values drop below 2000 με.

claims.claim: The proposed multimodal fault diagnosis algorithm achieved an average thermal runaway warning time of approximately 11 days across multiple TR vehicles.
claims.quote: The proposed algorithm successfully issued thermal runaway warnings 11, 10, and 8 days before the events occurred. Overall, the proposed algorithm demonstrates reproducibility in identifying vehicles at risk of thermal runaway, with an average warning time of approximately 11 days, thereby achieving a week-level thermal runaway warning.

claims.claim: For the TR vehicle, cell #76 triggered thermal runaway approximately 30 minutes after switching from charging to driving, followed by TR approximately 10 seconds later.
claims.quote: the voltage of cell #76 dropped sharply on the day of the incident, which occurred approximately 30 min after switching from charging to driving, then about 10 s later this vehicle experienced TR and the battery management system in the vehicle stopped recording data.

claims.claim: An anomaly in one cell (cell #50) caused abnormal behavior in adjacent parallel-connected cells within the same module, as evidenced by slightly elevated fault scores in cells #49–#60.
claims.quote: The abnormality level of cells #49–#60 was slightly higher than those of normal cells but did not surpass the threshold. This could be attributed to the slight differences in the parallel-connected cells within the same module caused by the abnormality in cell #50.

claims.claim: The multidimensional recursive algorithm reduced the average distance from normal cells to the centroid by 39.7%, from 1.0766 to 0.6488, and improved the discriminative ability for defective cells by an average of 7.34%.
claims.quote: the average distance from normal cells to the centroid decreased from 1.0766 to 0.6488, a reduction of 39.7 %... When analyzing other segments, we found that the overall average discriminative ability for defective cells improved by 7.34 %.

claims.claim: Using a single TSM feature alone is insufficient to set a unified threshold for faulty cell isolation, because the distinction between faulty and normal cells is often minor before thermal runaway occurs.
claims.quote: the differentiation between faulty cells and normal cells is relatively poor because the distinction between them is often minor before TR occurs. It is challenging to use a single feature to set a unified threshold for faulty cell isolation despite being able to distinguish anomalous cells from normal cells.

claims.claim: The SEI film begins to decompose at approximately 90°C, exposing the anode and its lithium-embedded carbon components to the electrolyte, which then reacts exothermically with the electrolyte, causing the temperature to further rise.
