claims.quote: As the temperature climbs to 90 ◦C, the SEI layer begins to disintegrate, exposing the anode and its lithium-embedded carbon components to the electrolyte, which then reacts exothermically with the electrolyte, causing the temperature to further rise

claims.claim: When the battery temperature exceeds 130°C, the diaphragm starts to melt and progressively fractures, and the interior of the battery is shorted by direct contact of the positive and negative electrodes.
claims.quote: When the temperature exceeds 130 ◦C, the diaphragm starts to melt and progressively fractures, and the interior of the battery is shorted by the direct contact of the positive and negative electrodes.

claims.claim: At temperatures up to 200°C, both the cathode and electrolyte start to break down and release significant amounts of toxic, dangerous, and combustible gases such as HF, hydrogen, carbon monoxide, and hydrocarbons.
claims.quote: Up to 200 ◦C, both the cathode and electrolyte start to break down and even interact with the adhesive, releasing a significant amount of toxic, dangerous, and combustible gases, such as HF, hydrogen, carbon monoxide, and hydrocarbons

claims.claim: In NCM and LFP cells, the release of flammable gas occurs approximately 1585 s and 987 s before the fire, respectively, indicating that gas signals provide earlier warning than temperature, voltage, and pressure signals.
claims.quote: Cui et al. [130] discovered that in NCM and LFP cells, the release of flammable gas occurs approximately 1585 s and 987 s before the fire, respectively. The results indicate that the unique gas signal can not only provide an early warning during thermal runaway but also provide an earlier warning than the temperature, voltage, and pressure signals.

claims.claim: When a gas signal is used as the benchmark for thermal runaway warning, an early warning can be given 7.05 min before thermal runaway peaks, compared to 5.96 min for voltage signals and only 0.5 min for temperature signals.
claims.quote: the figure indicates that when the gas signal is used as the benchmark, an early warning can be given 7.05 min before thermal runaway peaks, giving more time for escape and prevention, as opposed to when the voltage and temperature signals are used, which only allow for 5.96 min and 0.5 min of evacuation time, respectively.

claims.claim: For a battery at 100% SOC, the internal pressure buildup follows a power law as a function of time and reaches the critical pressure threshold of 1.5 MPa within 94 ms, after which the safety valve opens.
claims.quote: For a battery at 100 % SOC, this state reaches the critical pressure threshold of 1.5 MPa within only 94 ms. After that, the battery's safety valve opens through its outlet.

claims.claim: After the safety valve opens, the internal pressure drops sharply and approaches external atmospheric pressure within 0.03 ms.
claims.quote: Within 0.03 ms, the internal pressure approaches the external atmospheric pressure (Fig. 4(a)).

claims.claim: The pyrolysis gas jetting initiated at a maximum velocity of 450 m/s at the central outlet is completed in less than 2 milliseconds.
claims.quote: The initial peak velocity at the central outlet is 450 m/s, rising to the maximum value of about 500 m/s... This indicates that the initial jetting of the pyrolysis gas is completed in less than 2 milliseconds.

claims.claim: The initial jet fire lasts approximately 6 ms after safety valve rupture and lags behind the pyrolysis gas injection velocity due to accumulation of combustion heat.
claims.quote: The initial jet fire – which lasts around 6 ms after the safety valve rupture – lags behind the pyrolysis gas injection velocity and demonstrates pronounced downstream propagation.

claims.claim: At t = 94.05 ms, CO2 reaches its highest mass fraction of 70% above the safety valve nozzle during the initial jet fire combustion.
claims.quote: At this moment, CO2 exhibits the highest mass fraction of 70 %. Following this maximum, the mass fractions of combustible gases start to decrease gradually.

claims.claim: TR induced by slight overcharge cycles has a longer incubation period and involves heat accumulation, and once thermal runaway occurs, it poses greater hazards and spreads at a faster rate compared to TR induced by thermal or mechanical abuse.
claims.quote: In contrast with TR induced by thermal/mechanical abuse conditions, TR induced by slight overcharge cycles has a longer incubation period and involves a certain amount of heat accumulation. Once thermal runaway occurs, it poses greater hazards and spreads at a faster rate

claims.claim: When the SOC of a 1×5 NCM622 cell cluster was below 25%, no TR occurred within the cluster.
claims.quote: They found that when the SOC of the cell cluster was below 25 %, no TR occurred within the cluster.

claims.claim: In a 1×5 NCM622 cell cluster, total gas generation decreased from 17.48 mol to 6.17 mol when SOC was reduced from 100% to 50%.
claims.quote: the total gas generation decreased from 17.48 mol to 6.17 mol when the SOC was reduced from 100 % to 50 %

claims.claim: Using composite phase-change insulating materials completely inhibited thermal diffusion between lithium cells within a 1×4 cluster, achieving zero TRP within the component.
claims.quote: the use of composite phase-change insulating materials completely inhibited thermal diffusion between lithium cells within the cluster, achieving the goal of zero TRP within the component.

claims.claim: Compared to cells without insulation in a 1×4 LIB cluster, the average propagation time of TR increased by 5.27 times and 7.36 times for cells with different insulation materials.
claims.quote: Compared to cells without insulation, the average propagation time of TR increased by 5.27 times and 7.36 times for cells with different insulation materials.

claims.claim: Mechanical abuse accounts for 18% of EV accidents in China.
claims.quote: Mechanical abuse accounts for 18% of EV accidents in China.

claims.claim: The solid electrolyte interface (SEI) large-scale decomposition is triggered when the cell temperature exceeds 130 °C.
claims.quote: The solid electrolyte interface (SEI) large-scale decomposition is triggered when the cell temperature exceeds 130 °C.

claims.claim: The anode granular layer becomes denser as the SOC increases, which leads to an increase in its stiffness and strength.
claims.quote: The anode granular layer becomes denser as the SOC increases, which leads to an increase in its stiffness and strength.

claims.claim: In the mechanical-electrical-thermal coupling model, when the distance between positive and negative current collectors is less than threshold r, a short-circuit resistor Rs is used to simulate the ISC; the higher the SOC, the sooner the ISC occurs, so r is set to increase as the SOC increases.
claims.quote: When the distance is less than a set threshold r, a short-circuit resistor Rs is used instead of the equivalent circuit to simulate the ISC. The higher the SOC, the sooner the ISC of battery, so r is set to increase as the SOC increases.

claims.claim: The simulation model cannot simulate the force-electrical-thermal response of the battery under axial compression due to model limitations.
claims.quote: due to the limitation of the simulation model, this model cannot simulate the force–electrical–thermal response of the battery under axial compression.

claims.claim: A broad-scale internal short circuit event can cause cell temperature to rapidly rise to 300°C or higher, leading to cathode material decomposition and massive oxygen release.
claims.quote: This broad-scale ISC event can cause the cell temperature to rapidly rise to 300°C or higher, leading to cathode material decomposition and massive oxygen release.

claims.claim: When cell pressure reaches its designed critical level, the safety valve activates, releasing gases and initiating the initial venting event.
claims.quote: Once the cell pressure reaches its designed critical level, the safety valve will activate, releasing gases and initiating the initial venting event.

claims.claim: Heat release rates during fire propagation at the system level can rapidly escalate to the MW scale, potentially igniting other combustible components and leading to catastrophic fire incidents.
claims.quote: The heat release rates (HRRs) during fire propagation at the system level can rapidly escalate to the MW scale, potentially igniting other combustible components and leading to catastrophic fire incidents.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway performance is influenced by materials, state of charge (SOC), and state of health (SOH), meaning thermodynamic parameters obtained from calorimetry tests are specific to one chemical system or state.
claims.quote: thermodynamic parameters obtained from calorimetry tests are specific to one chemical system or state, as TR performance is influenced by materials, state of charge (SOC), and state of health (SOH).

claims.claim: Chemical crosstalk between cathode oxygen and anode can directly generate heat and trigger thermal runaway without severe internal short circuit.
claims.quote: chemical crosstalk between cathode oxygen and anode can directly generate heat, triggering TR without severe ISC.

claims.claim: Even if an internal short circuit is induced, the joule heat from ISC may be limited by sharply increased battery resistance, thus contributing minimally to thermal runaway heat generation.
claims.quote: even if ISC is induced, the joule heat from ISC may be limited by sharply increased battery resistance, thus contributing minimally to TR heat generation.

claims.claim: When battery voltage exceeds 4.2 V during overcharging of lithium iron phosphate batteries, the temperature begins to change, and within a certain voltage range the temperature changes slowly.
claims.quote: Comparing the temperature at the center of the battery surface with the voltage between the two poles, when the voltage exceeds 4.2 V, the temperature begins to change, and within a certain voltage range, the temperature changes slowly.

claims.claim: When battery voltage exceeds 5.2 V during overcharging, the surface temperature begins to rise sharply, marking the transition to the early-mid stage of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: When the voltage is greater than 5.2 V, the temperature begins to rise sharply. When the voltage exceeds 5.2 V or the temperature exceeds 50 ◦C, it is defined as the early-mid stage of TR.

claims.claim: At higher charging rates during overcharging at 50% SOC, the slow temperature rise stage is shorter and thermal runaway occurs faster inside the battery.
claims.quote: the shorter the slow temperature rise stage, the faster TR occurs inside the battery, especially at higher charging rates.

claims.claim: Gas generation was detected during overcharging experiments on commercial lithium iron phosphate batteries when the battery surface temperature reached 53°C and the SOC reached 120%.
claims.quote: Fernandes et al. [19] conducted overcharging experiments on commercial lithium iron phosphate batteries and found that gas generation was detected when the battery surface temperature reached 53 ◦C and the battery State of Charge (SOC) reached 120 %.

claims.claim: Voltage-based thermal runaway detection is limited because when the voltage signal undergoes a sudden change, thermal runaway inside the battery has already occurred for some time.
claims.quote: when the voltage signal undergoes a sudden change, thermal runaway inside the battery has already occurred for some time, making it difficult to achieve very early warning based solely on a single voltage change.

claims.claim: Increasing FRPCM thickness from 1 mm to 2 mm and 3 mm (with constant 1 mm AEGF) reduced the maximum temperature of cell 2 by 67.1°C and 113.8°C respectively, compared to the 1 mm FRPCM battery module.
claims.quote: Compared to the 1 mm FRPCM BM, the highest temperature of cell 2 within the 2 mm and 3 mm FRPCM BMs was decreased by 67.1 ◦C and 113.8 ◦C, respectively.

claims.claim: Increasing FRPCM thickness extended the TR interval between cells 1 and 2, with time intervals of 184 s, 291 s, and 533 s for 1 mm, 2 mm, and 3 mm FRPCM respectively (with constant 1 mm AEGF).
claims.quote: The time intervals for TR between cells 1 and 2 were 184 s, 291 s, and 533 s, while those between cells 2 and 3 were 137 s, 280 s and 493 s.

claims.claim: In Test-6 (3 mm FRPCM + 2 mm AEGF) and Test-7 (1 mm FRPCM + 3 mm AEGF), thermal runaway propagation was completely blocked, with only cell 1 undergoing TR.
claims.quote: Both the structural materials in Test-6 and Test-7 block the TRP. In Test-6, the introduction of a thicker layer of FRPCM led to only cell 1 undergoing TR.

claims.claim: The TR interval between cells 2 and 3 was consistently shorter than the interval between cells 1 and 2, because cell 3 absorbed heat from the heating plate and TR of cell 1 before cell 2 underwent TR.
claims.quote: the TR interval between cells 2 and 3 is consistently shorter compared to the interval between cells 1 and 2. This phenomenon arises from the fact that, preceding the TR of cell 3, the heating plate and the TR of the first cell supplied a considerable amount of heat to the entire BM. Moreover, cell 3 absorbed some of this heat before cell 2 underwent TR, consequently reducing the interval between the final two cells.

claims.claim: Increasing AEGF thickness improved thermal insulation and extended TR intervals between cells, but did not necessarily reduce maximum battery temperature and could even cause temperature increases despite the same FRPCM thickness.
claims.quote: an increase in the thickness of the AEGF results in improved heat insulation between the cells. However, it should be noted that this increase in thickness does not necessarily lead to a better reduction in the maximum battery temperature. In fact, in some cases, it can even cause a temperature increase despite the same thickness of the FRPCM.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway propagation interval within a module is reduced by 72%, from 611 s to 176 s, indicating an accelerating trend due to heat accumulation once thermal runaway initiates.
claims.quote: the thermal runaway propagation interval is markedly reduced by 72%, from 611 s to 176 s, indicating that controlling the initial thermal spread within the module is more critical than mitigating thermal propagation between modules

claims.claim: During thermal runaway propagation, energy dissipated via liquid cooling plates accounts for more than 70% of the total energy, far exceeding heat transfer through neighboring battery surfaces and tab connectors.
claims.quote: during thermal runaway propagation, the energy dissipated via the liquid cooling plates accounts for more than 70% of the total energy, far exceeding the heat transfer through neighboring battery surfaces and the tab connector

claims.claim: Replacing polystyrene insulation with aerogel decreases heat transfer through the rear surface of the battery by 55.3%, significantly slowing thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: Replacing polystyrene with aerogel decreases heat transfer through the rear surface of the battery by 55.3%, significantly slowing the progression of thermal runaway propagation

claims.claim: Using a single piece of 13 mm thick aerogel positioned between the third and fourth cells accelerates the overall thermal runaway propagation time by 46.2% compared to evenly distributed aerogel.
claims.quote: using a single piece of 13 mm thick aerogel accelerates the overall TR propagation time by 46.2%

claims.claim: Replacing the air gap with aerogel between modules delays the thermal runaway onset moment for adjacent modules by only 36 s and 49 s because over 70% of heat flows through the bottom cooling plate from module to module.
claims.quote: Replacing the air gap with aerogel will slightly delay 36 s and 49 s of the TR onset moment for module 5 and module 7, respectively. The insulation performance of aerogel is limited since over 70% of heat flows through the bottom cooling plate from module to module

claims.claim: The average maximum temperature during thermal runaway of NMC-811 cells was 899.3 °C, significantly higher than that of LFP cells at 524.4 °C.
claims.quote: The average maximum temperature of the NMC-811 cells is 𝑇 𝑇 𝑅,𝑚𝑎𝑥,𝑁𝑀𝐶811 = 899.3 ◦C (𝜎𝑁𝑀𝐶811 = 38.98 ◦C), and therefore significantly higher than that of the LFP cells with 𝑇 𝑇 𝑅,𝑚𝑎𝑥,𝐿𝐹 𝑃 = 524.4 ◦C (𝜎𝐿𝐹 𝑃 = 5.94 ◦C). The reasons for this are a higher reaction enthalpy and a lower thermal mass than the LFP cells

claims.claim: The maximum temperatures of NMC-811 cells during thermal runaway exceed the melting temperature of aluminum (660 °C), requiring adjacent components to consist of temperature-resistant materials like steel to ensure structural integrity, whereas lighter materials like aluminum can be used in LFP batteries.
claims.quote: The maximum temperatures of the NMC-811 cells exceed the melting temperature of aluminum (𝑇𝑚𝑒𝑙𝑡,𝐴𝑙 = 660 ◦C [52]), requiring that adjacent components of the cell module or battery housing consist of temperature-resistant materials like steel to ensure structural integrity and to avoid unwanted venting gas leaks. By contrast, lighter materials like aluminum can be used in LFP batteries.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway reaction in NMC-811 cells is nine times faster in terms of cell energy compared to LFP cells, with NMC-811 having a reaction velocity of 20.33 Wh/s versus 2.19 Wh/s for LFP.
claims.quote: The thermal runaway reaction in the cell is, therefore, eight times faster in terms of capacity and nine times faster in terms of cell energy, compared to LFP cells with a thermal runaway reaction velocity of 0.66 Ah s−1 and 2.19 Wh s−1, respectively.

claims.claim: The overall thermal runaway propagation velocity in the LFP module is five times lower than in the NMC-811 module, giving LFP a clear safety advantage.
claims.quote: Overall, the total propagation velocity remains lower by a factor of five, which gives the LFP module a clear safety advantage over the NMC-811 module.

claims.claim: NMC-811 cells experienced an average mass loss of 45.8% during thermal runaway, leading to reduced mechanical integrity and increased thermal contact resistance, while LFP cells had a significantly smaller average mass loss of 21.1%, maintaining mechanical integrity.
claims.quote: the NMC-811 cells experience a mass loss of 39.6–48.7%. This leads to a reduced mechanical integrity and clamping force up to small gaps between the cells, which results in higher thermal contact resistances and slower propagation. On the contrary, mechanical integrity is maintained in the LFP cells with significantly smaller mass losses of 20.9–21.6%.

claims.claim: The venting gas of NMC-811 cells ignited during thermal runaway because the ignition temperature of C2H4 (440 °C) was exceeded at the safety vents, while the venting gas of LFP cells did not ignite as the ignition temperature was not reached.
claims.quote: According to the data from the thermocouples on the safety vents at the NMC-811 cell in Fig. 13(c), the lowest ignition temperature of the venting gas components of C2H4 (440 °C [72]) was exceeded several times, so the cell itself provides enough heat for ignition and acts as an ignition source, which means that ignition of NMC-811 cells is unavoidable in the presence of oxygen. In the case of LFP cells, however, the ignition temperature is not reached, as can be seen in Fig. 13(d), which means that the venting gas does not ignite.

claims.claim: The venting gas temperature during NMC-811 thermal runaway exceeds the melting temperature of PE (100 °C) and PTFE (320 °C), which are common electrical insulation materials for high-voltage busbars, creating a risk of arcing in the high-voltage system.
claims.quote: the venting path should also be insulated from the electrical path or all high-voltage components, as the venting gas temperature exceeds the melting temperature of common electrical insulation materials for high-voltage busbars, as shown in Fig. 13(c) and (d) for the insulation materials PE (𝑇𝑚𝑒𝑙𝑡,𝑃 𝐸 = 100 °C [73]) and PTFE (𝑇𝑚𝑒𝑙𝑡,𝑃 𝑇 𝐹 𝐸 = 320 °C [74]). The lack of electrical insulation can, in turn, lead to arcing in the high-voltage system and thus increase the hazardous potential.

claims.claim: At the pack level, the gravimetric energy density advantage of ternary (NMC/NCA) batteries over LFP batteries decreases from 34% at cell level to 16% at pack level, because LFP batteries have lower integration losses (20%) compared to ternary batteries (37%).
claims.quote: At the cell level, the average gravimetric energy densities are 252 Wh kg−1 for ternary and 167 Wh kg−1 for LFP batteries. This 34% advantage can be partially transferred to the pack level. With an average energy density of 158 Wh kg−1 for ternary and 133 Wh kg−1 for LFP batteries including all integration and safety measures, the advantage is still present but decreases to 16% because the losses due to the integration of the cells into the battery pack are lower for LFP batteries at 20% than for ternary batteries at 37%.

claims.claim: At the pack level, the volumetric energy density advantage of ternary batteries over LFP is completely eliminated, with average pack-level volumetric energy densities of 241 Wh/L for ternary and 243 Wh/L for LFP, due to higher integration losses of 62% for ternary versus 40% for LFP.
claims.quote: With an average energy density of 241 Wh L−1 for ternary and 243 Wh L−1 for LFP batteries including all integration and safety measures, the average energy densities at pack level are almost identical due to the integration losses in energy density of 62% for ternary batteries and 40% for LFP batteries.

claims.claim: Side wall ruptures occurred in NMC-811 cell cans due to temperatures above the melting point of aluminum, causing unintentional gas leakage and preventing venting exclusively via intended channels, while no structural loss or side wall ruptures were observed in the LFP module.
claims.quote: it can be seen that although the initial venting occurs through the intended vent in the lid assembly, side wall ruptures also happen due to the high temperatures above the melting point of the aluminum cell can... In contrast, Fig. 15(b) and (d) show no visually detectable structural loss of the LFP cell module. All cells released venting gas exclusively through the safety vents, and no side wall ruptures are evident.

claims.claim: The five-minute thermal propagation safety requirement of GB 38031-2020 can likely be met without additional measures for LFP cells, as only 107.25 Ah and 360 Wh would undergo thermal runaway in that period, whereas the complete five-cell NMC-811 setup would fully propagate within the same period.
claims.quote: Regarding the five-minute requirement for the thermal propagation test in the GB 38031-2020 standard [9], these requirements can easily be achieved with LFP cells, as only 107.25 Ah and 360 Wh will go in thermal runaway during this period with a measured average propagation velocity of 0.36 Ah s−1 and 1.20 Wh s−1. For this reason, the safety requirement can probably even be fulfilled without other measures. On the other hand, the complete, tested five-cell NMC-811 setup would propagate through in this period.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway triggering temperature for NMC-811 cells was 371.6 °C at the front surface with 33.9 °C at the back, while LFP cells triggered at a significantly higher temperature of 436.6 °C at the front with 41.6 °C at the back, due to the higher thermal stability of the LFP cathode.
claims.quote: This point was reached in the NMC-811 module at a surface temperature of 371.6 °C. The thermal runaway of the first LFP cell was reached significantly later with a temperature of 436.6 °C, which can be justified by the significantly higher thermal stability of the LFP cathode due to their strong and thermally stable P-O bonds and inhibition of oxygen loss... the energy balance between heat generation and dissipation within the cell comes out of equilibrium at temperatures of 371.6 °C at the front and 33.9 °C at the back of the NMC-811 cell. Analogous, the LFP cell goes into thermal runaway at temperatures of 436.6 °C at the front and 41.6 °C at the back of the cell.

claims.claim: The cell voltage is not always a suitable thermal runaway detection criterion for slowly propagating cell chemistries or cells connected in parallel, because the voltage does not completely collapse and may remain within the normal permissible operating range.
claims.quote: This phenomenon illustrates the complexity of detecting thermal runaway via the cell voltage, as the voltage does not completely collapse in the case of slowly propagating cell chemistries or cells connected in parallel. The measured values are still in the normal permissible operating range of the cell, making it difficult to distinguish them from an intentionally high current load. The cell voltage is, therefore, not always a suitable detection criterion, and other redundant detection methods should be taken into account

claims.claim: The exponential increase in time span between successive thermal runaways in the NMC-811 module is attributable only to the pause time component, not to the internal thermal runaway reaction time, due to increasing thermal contact resistance from mass ejection reducing mechanical integrity.
claims.quote: Considering the internal propagation times and the subsequent pause time, this exponential increase is only found in the pause time of the NMC-811 cells, which reinforces the assumption.

claims.claim: The total venting gas production rate for NMC-811 cells is significantly higher than for LFP cells, with NMC-811 having a mean production rate of 2.44 L/Ah compared to 0.56 L/Ah for LFP cells, allowing pressure relief elements and venting channels in LFP battery systems to be dimensioned significantly smaller.
claims.quote: the mean production rate for NMC-811 cell chemistries of 2.44 l ⋅ Ah−1 is used for the following calculation. For LFP cells, significantly lower values between 0.57–1.12 l Ah−1 have been reported in the literature... the pressure relief elements and venting channels in LFP battery systems can be dimensioned significantly smaller than in NMC battery systems, as the flow rate is significantly lower and can be easily compensated.

claims.claim: The battery module without thermal insulation had an average thermal spreading time of only 44.6 s per cell and a complete thermal spreading time of 134 s for the module.
claims.quote: the thermal spreading of the battery module (No.1 experiment) without insulation was very fast, with an average spreading time D of only 44.6 s for each cell, and a complete thermal spreading time D of 134 s for the module.

claims.claim: Using sponge ceramic nanofiber insulation increased the average thermal spreading time per cell by 5.27 times compared to the module without insulation, while nano-glass fiber insulation increased it by 7.36 times.
claims.quote: The average thermal spread time of the module with sponge ceramic insulation (No.2 experiment) increased by 5.27 times compared to the module without insulation. The average thermal spread time of the module with nano-glass fiber insulation (No.3 experiment) increased by 7.36 times compared to the module without insulation.

claims.claim: Nanofiber insulation layers slow down thermal transfer between cells but cannot reduce it sufficiently to prevent thermal runaway in neighboring cells, so the zero-spreading effect cannot be achieved.
claims.quote: the nanofiber insulation layer in the module only slows down the thermal transfer. But it is not able to reduce the thermal transfer between the two cells. Eventually, the temperature of the neighboring non-runaway cell will still continue to rise, and thermal runaway will eventually occur.

claims.claim: The average spreading time per cell with ceramic aerogel insulation was 756 s and with pre-oxidized silk aerogel was 1045 s, representing increases of 102% and 180% respectively compared to nano-glass fiber insulation.
claims.quote: The average spreading time of each battery was 756 s and 1045 s. And the complete thermal spreading time of the whole module was 2269 s and 3137 s. The average spreading time of the module with ceramic aerogel insulation (No. 4 experiment) increased by 102 % compared to the module with nanofiber glass insulation (No. 3 experiment). The average spreading time of the module with pre-oxidized filament-reinforced aerogel insulation (No. 5 experiment) increased by 180 % compared to the module with nanofiber glass insulation (No. 3 experiment).

claims.claim: Using composite phase change insulation (sponge ceramic + PCM or nano glass fiber + PCM) extended the thermal spreading interval between the first and second cells to an infinite length, achieving zero spreading of thermal runaway in the battery module.
claims.quote: In the battery module with sponge ceramic + PCM or nano-glass fiber + PCM composite phase change thermal insulation layer, the thermal spread interval D1, 2 between the first and second batteries was extended to an infinite length. Except for the first battery, no thermal runaway occurred in the other batteries. That is, the zero-spreading effect of thermal runaway of the battery module was achieved.

claims.claim: A convective heat transfer coefficient exceeding 40 W/m2-K is capable of entirely suppressing thermal runaway propagation in densely packed batteries under 2C-rate discharge conditions.
claims.quote: the convective environment with a heat dissipation coefficient exceeding 40 W/m2-K (corresponding to a velocity of 1 m/s in this study) can entirely suppress TRP

claims.claim: Increasing ambient pressure from 1 bar to 2 bar slowed thermal runaway propagation time from 634 s to 670 s, and completely inhibited propagation at 3 bar, under 3C discharge rate and 0.5 m/s flow speed.
claims.quote: the TRP time slowed from 634 s at 1 bar to 670 s at 2 bar, and was completely inhibited at a pressure of 3 bar. This is attributed to the increase in fluid density with ambient pressure, leading to higher heat transfer capability.

claims.claim: Nitrogen exhibited superior heat dissipation capabilities compared to air and argon gas, resulting in lower risk of thermal runaway propagation, while argon exhibited the lowest effectiveness due to its lower heat capacity.
claims.quote: the cooling system using argon gas exhibited the lowest effectiveness compared to air and nitrogen, primarily due to its lower heat capacity. Despite argon gas having higher density, its lower heat capacity exerts a more significant influence on the cooling rate. Nitrogen, on the other hand, exhibits superior capabilities in reducing the RTRP.

claims.claim: SEI decomposition begins at 74°C and serves as the initial trigger for thermal runaway; if this heat is dissipated before initiating subsequent self-heating reactions, the battery is less susceptible to thermal runaway even under abusive conditions.
claims.quote: The simulation results indicate that the heat generated by SEI decomposition, occurring at 74°C, serves as the initial trigger for TR. If this heat is dissipated to the surroundings before initiating the self-heating process through subsequent reactions, the battery is less susceptible to TR, even under abusive conditions.

claims.claim: When RTRP falls below 70%, thermal runaway cannot propagate to adjacent cells; it is recommended to keep RTRP below 50% for high-safety design requirements.
claims.quote: RTRP = 100 % characterizes the occurrence of flames throughout the entire battery module, and when RTRP < 70 %, there is no propagation of flames to neighboring cells. However, it is preferable for RTRP to remain below 50 % for enhanced battery module safety.

claims.claim: When the temperature exceeds 120°C, the melamine-urea-formaldehyde resin shell of the hydrated microcapsule ruptures and releases the encapsulated Ba(OH)2·8H2O core material.
claims.quote: Once the temperature exceeds 120 ◦C, the organic shell ruptures and releases the encapsulated core material.

claims.claim: Using 4 g/Ah of the Ba(OH)2·8H2O microcapsules reduces the total reaction enthalpy of full battery components by 24.61% as measured by DSC.
claims.quote: Differential scanning calorimetry results indicate that only 4 g/Ah of the microcapsules can reduce the total reaction enthalpy by 24.61 %.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature during thermal runaway was reduced by 106.2°C when 4 g/Ah of Ba(OH)2·8H2O microcapsules were incorporated into NCM811/Gr pouch cells, as measured by ARC tests.
claims.quote: Accelerating rate calorimeter tests indicate that the maximum temperature during thermal runaway has been reduced by 106.2 ◦C.

claims.claim: Increasing microcapsule dosage from 0 g/Ah to 6 g/Ah progressively reduced maximum thermal runaway temperature, but with diminishing returns; a marginal decrease of only 10.1°C was observed from 4 g/Ah to 6 g/Ah.
claims.quote: However, only a marginal decrease of 10.1 ◦C was observed from 4 g/Ah to 6 g/Ah, suggesting diminishing returns.

claims.claim: Increasing microcapsule dosage to 6 g/Ah unexpectedly reduced the thermal runaway trigger time to 1777.8 s compared to 2343.4 s at 4 g/Ah, attributed to excessive microcapsule content accelerating low-temperature side reactions near 120°C involving lithiated graphite.
claims.quote: However, increasing the dosage to 6 g/Ah unexpectedly reduced the trigger time to 1777.8 s. This phenomenon is attributed to excessive microcapsule content accelerating low-temperature side reactions near 120 ◦C, such as those involving lithiated graphite.

claims.claim: Direct addition of Ba(OH)2·8H2O without encapsulation caused irreversible capacity fading within the first 5 cycles, with capacity retention dropping to 5.56% by the 9th cycle, rendering the battery unusable.
claims.quote: The results showed irreversible capacity fading within the first 5 cycles, with the capacity retention rate dropping to 5.56 % by the 9th cycle, at which point the battery was deemed unusable.

claims.claim: The presence of hydrated microcapsules (An+Mp) reduced the reaction enthalpy of 100% SOC lithiated graphite anode from 648.32 J/g to 453.17 J/g, a decrease of 30.1%, by reacting water released from microcapsules with lithiated graphite.
claims.quote: in the presence of hydrated microcapsules (An+Mp), the heat generation of the graphite anode is significantly reduced, with the reaction enthalpy being 453.17 J/g, a decrease of 30.1 %.

claims.claim: After adding hydrated microcapsules to the anode-electrolyte mixture, no significant exothermic peaks appeared after 200°C, indicating that consumption of lithiated graphite by water from microcapsules significantly weakened subsequent reactions between lithiated graphite and electrolyte or binder.
claims.quote: no significant exothermic peaks appear after 200 ◦C. This indicates that the addition of hydrated microcapsules effectively modulates the sequence of thermal runaway reactions, consuming reactants through low-heat-generation reactions in advance.

claims.claim: Under lateral heating abuse conditions, the addition of microcapsules reduced the maximum temperature rise rate from 390°C/s to 52.75°C/s, a reduction of 86.47%.
claims.quote: Under the lateral heating experimental condition, the maximum temperature rise rate of the base sample was 390 ◦Cs(cid:0) 1, while that of the battery with microcapsules added was 52.75 ◦Cs(cid:0) 1, a reduction of 86.47 %.

claims.claim: The battery assembled with microcapsules achieved a capacity retention rate of 97.33% after 225 cycles at a charge rate of 1/3C and discharge rate of 1C, compared to 97.68% for the conventional battery.
claims.quote: after 255 cycles of charging at 1/3C rate and discharging at 1C rate, the discharge curves of the conventional battery and the battery with implanted hydrated microcapsules almost coincide. The capacity retention rate of the conventional battery is 97.68 %, while that of the battery with implanted hydrated microcapsules is 97.33 %.

claims.claim: The hybrid cooling plate with the optimal configuration reduced maximum battery temperature (Tmax) by 1.4°C and temperature difference (Tdif) by 0.83°C compared to an aluminum cooling plate with the same structure during 2C discharge.
claims.quote: the hybrid cooling plate with relatively optimal configuration reduced Tmax by 1.4 ◦C and Tdif by 0.83 ◦C compared to an aluminum cooling plate with the same structure during 2C discharge.

claims.claim: Under vehicle greenhouse thermal soak conditions, the hybrid cooling plate maintained battery temperature below 31.95°C, demonstrating a 34.7% reduction in temperature rise compared to the aluminum cooling plate.
claims.quote: Under vehicle greenhouse thermal soak conditions, the hybrid cooling plate maintained the battery temperature below 31.95 ◦C, demonstrating a 34.7 % reduction in temperature rise compared to the aluminum cooling plate.

claims.claim: In thermal runaway scenarios, the PCM-fin structure effectively isolated heat propagation, preventing adjacent cells from exceeding 100°C.
claims.quote: In thermal runaway scenarios, the PCM-fin structure effectively isolated heat propagation, preventing adjacent cells from exceeding 100 ◦C.

claims.claim: Under aluminum cooling plate conditions, thermal runaway in the faulty No. 6 cell propagated to adjacent No. 7 cell, which reached a maximum temperature of 801°C at around 95 seconds.
claims.quote: Due to the high thermal conductivity of the aluminum plate, the heat generated by the faulty No. 6 cell is rapidly conducted to adjacent No. 7 cell, causing their temperatures to rise beyond the thermal runaway threshold. The maximum temperature of 801 ◦C is reached around 95 s, indicating that thermal runaway has spread between cells.

claims.claim: PCM with high thermal conductivity is beneficial for reducing battery temperature, but it may lead to thermal runaway propagation; this contradiction can be resolved by enhancing the intensity of auxiliary cooling.
claims.quote: Zhang et al. [28] used hybrid PCM and liquid cooling to avoid thermal runaway propagation, pointing out that the PCM with high thermal conductivity is beneficial for reducing battery temperature, but it may lead to thermal runaway propagation. This contradiction can be resolved by enhancing the intensity of auxiliary cooling.

claims.claim: Future 628 Ah LiFePO4 batteries will release at least 2 kg of electrolyte per cell during thermal runaway, compared to approximately 1 kg for current 314 Ah LFP batteries.
claims.quote: The existing 314 Ah LFP battery contains approximately 1 kg of electrolyte, while the 628 Ah LFP battery is estimated to contain about 2 kg of electrolyte.

claims.claim: Under identical heating conditions, the thick-structure 628 Ah battery triggered thermal runaway earlier at 395 s, whereas the thin-structure design exhibited delayed onset at 487 s.
claims.quote: the thick-structure battery triggered TR earlier at 395 s, whereas the thin-structure design exhibited delayed onset, undergoing TR at 487 s.

claims.claim: The thick-structure 628 Ah battery exhibited a marginally higher front-surface peak temperature (+9°C) than the thin-structure counterpart but demonstrated a substantially lower back-surface temperature (−53°C), indicating more complex thermal dynamics in the thick-structure battery during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the thick-structure battery exhibited a marginally higher front-surface peak temperature (+9°C) than its thin-structure counterpart, it demonstrated a substantially lower back-surface temperature (−53°C), suggesting superior internal temperature uniformity in the thin-structure design but more complex thermal dynamics in the thick-structure battery during TR.

claims.claim: A centralized single insulation layer of 2.11 mm thickness completely suppresses internal thermal propagation in thick-structure 628 Ah batteries, while thicknesses below this threshold only delay propagation and produce staged thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Computational analysis identified 2.11 mm as the critical thickness for thermal insulation materials to completely suppress internal thermal propagation in batteries, while thicknesses below this threshold could delay thermal propagation and achieve staged TR of JRs.

claims.claim: For thin-structure 628 Ah batteries, a single centralized insulation layer of 2.85 mm is required to completely suppress internal thermal propagation, whereas thicknesses below this value enable staged thermal runaway development.
claims.quote: while a thickness of 2.85 mm completely suppresses internal thermal propagation, thicknesses below this threshold enable staged TR within the battery.

claims.claim: Three distributed insulation layers significantly impede heat dissipation in both thick and thin 628 Ah battery cells, whereas a single centralized insulation layer increases maximum internal temperature by only 0.11°C and 0.07°C for thick and thin cells respectively after 2000 s of natural cooling, indicating negligible impact on thermal dissipation.
claims.quote: the single-layer insulation increases temperatures by merely 0.11°C and 0.07°C for thick and thin cells, respectively, compared to the non-insulated baseline, indicating negligible impact on thermal dissipation when employing a single insulation layer.

claims.claim: Thin 628 Ah batteries require 18.7% higher energy to trigger thermal runaway compared to thick-structure 628 Ah batteries, demonstrating superior thermal safety performance from an internal heat dissipation perspective.
claims.quote: Thin battery structures demonstrate superior thermal homogeneity and require 18.7% higher energy to trigger TR compared to thick structures.

claims.claim: The model predictions for 628 Ah batteries are based on 314 Ah battery thermal runaway characteristics as input, which may lead to underestimation of thermal runaway severity, required insulation thicknesses, and peak temperatures in 628 Ah cells.
claims.quote: this approach may lead to an underestimation of the thermal runaway severity of 628 Ah batteries... the model likely underestimates: TR temperatures and Required insulation thicknesses.

claims.claim: When insulation thickness exceeds a critical threshold of typically 2–3 mm per UL 9540A testing, thermal runaway containment between cells becomes achievable, but relying solely on thermal insulation and cooling cannot guarantee 100% prevention of thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: when insulation thickness exceeds a critical threshold (typically 2-3 mm per UL 9540 A testing), TR containment becomes achievable. However, relying solely on thermal insulation and cooling cannot guarantee 100% prevention of TR propagation.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway, LiFePO4 batteries primarily eject gas-liquid phase mixtures accounting for about 20% of total cell mass, of which only 2% consists of non-condensable flammable gases while the remaining 18% is electrolyte mixed with internal battery materials.
claims.quote: During TR, these batteries primarily eject gas-liquid phase mixtures, accounting for about 20% of the total cell mass. Of this 20%, only 2% consists of non-condensable flammable gases, while the remaining 18% is electrolyte mixed with internal battery materials.

claims.claim: LIBs in the non-immersed group underwent thermal runaway with combustion and explosion, while LIBs in three immersed groups only released gas without TR, burning, or explosion.
claims.quote: the LIBs in the non-immersed group underwent TR with combustion and explosion, while those in three immersed groups only released a certain amount of gas without TR or burning and explosion occurring throughout the experiment.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature rise rate (TRRmax) of LIBs in the non-immersed group was 16.4°C/s, far above the TR critical threshold of 1°C/s, while TRRmax values in dimethyl silicone oil, No.15 hydraulic oil, and No.10 transformer oil immersed groups were 0.2°C/s, 0.4°C/s, and 0.2°C/s respectively, all below the TR threshold.
claims.quote: the TRRmax of the LIBs in the non-immersed group were 16.4 ◦C/s, which was far above the critical threshold (1 ◦C/s), indicating that TR occurred. In contrast, the TRRmax of the LIBs in the dimethyl silicone oil, No.15 hydraulic oil, No.10 transformer oil-immersed groups were 0.2 ◦C/s, 0.4 ◦C/s, and 0.2 ◦C/s, respectively, and thus did not experience the TR.

claims.claim: Compared with the non-immersed group, the maximum surface temperature (Tmax) of LIBs in dimethyl silicone oil, No.10 transformer oil, and No.15 hydraulic oil immersed groups reduced by 68.89%, 65.38%, and 69.37%, respectively.
claims.quote: Compared with the non-immersed group, the Tmax in the dimethyl silicone oil, No.10 transformer oil, and No.15 hydraulic oil-immersed groups reduced by 68.89%, 65.38%, and 69.37%, respectively, indicating that the thermal risk of the LIBs decreased in immersed conditions.

claims.claim: The safety valve opening time (topen) for LIBs in No.10 transformer oil immersion was delayed by 145.03% compared to natural air cooling, while dimethyl silicone oil and No.15 hydraulic oil delayed topen by 107.3% and 91.3%, respectively.
claims.quote: Compared with natural air cooling, The topen for the dimethyl silicone oil, No.10 transformer oil, and No.15 hydraulic oil-immersed groups delayed by 107.3%, 145.03% and 91.3%

claims.claim: The peak CO concentration in the non-immersed group was 4874 ppm, exceeding the maximum exposure limit of 447 ppm, while immersed groups in dimethyl silicone oil, No.10 transformer oil, and No.15 hydraulic oil reduced peak CO by 87.6x, 86x, and 40x respectively, all below the maximum exposure limit.
claims.quote: The peak concentration of CO released by LIBs under experimental conditions of #1 to #4 were 4874 ppm, 55 ppm, 56 ppm, and 119 ppm, respectively. The peak concentration of CO in non-immersed group was much higher than the maximum exposure limit concentration (447 ppm)... Compared with the non-immersed group, the peak concentration of CO released by LIBs in the dimethyl silicone oil, No.10 transformer oil, and No.15 hydraulic oil-immersed groups reduced by 87.6, 86, and 40 times, respectively, all of which was significantly lower than the maximum exposure limit concentration.

claims.claim: As immersion height increased from 0 mm to 75 mm using dimethyl silicone oil, peak CO concentration decreased from 4874 ppm to 55 ppm (98.9% reduction), peak H2 decreased from 4064 ppm to 45 ppm (98.9% reduction), and peak HF decreased from 2.6 ppm to 0.4 ppm (84.6% reduction).
claims.quote: the concentration of CO at its peak declined from 4874 ppm in the non-immersed group to 470 ppm at 17.5 mm, 297 ppm at 35 mm, and 55 ppm at 75 mm immersion groups, corresponding to reductions of 90.4%, 93.9%, and 98.9%, respectively. Similarly, the concentration of H₂ at its peak declined from 4064 ppm in the non-immersed group to 384 ppm, 228 ppm, and 45 ppm under 17.5 mm, 35 mm, and 75 mm immersion groups, representing decreases of 90.6%, 94.4%, and 98.9%. For HF, the peak measured value also fell from 2.6 ppm to 1.2 ppm at 17.5 mm, 1.1 ppm at 35 mm, and 0.4 ppm at 75 mm, equating to reductions of 53.8%, 57.7%, and 84.6%.

claims.claim: As immersion height increased from 0 mm to 75 mm, LIB mass loss decreased from 41% to 4.3%, representing a nearly 90% reduction, demonstrating that dimethyl silicone oil immersion cooling suppressed internal pyrolysis reactions.
claims.quote: The LIBs in the non-immersed group experienced severe reactions with 41% mass loss. For the partially immersed groups (17.5 mm and 35 mm), mass loss of the LIBs decreased to 8.5% and 5.6%, respectively. In fully immersed group, the LIBs exhibited mass losses of only 4.3%. As the immersion height increased from 0 mm to 75 mm, the mass loss dropped sharply by nearly 90%

claims.claim: EX (combustible hydrocarbon gases) was completely eliminated (100% reduction) under full immersion at 75 mm, while at 17.5 mm and 35 mm immersion heights EX was reduced by 88.9% and 92.6% respectively compared to the non-immersed group.
claims.quote: The EX released by the LIBs reached the peak concentration of 27% LEL in the non-immersed (0 mm) group, declining to 3% LEL, 2% LEL, and 0% LEL in the 17.5 mm, 35 mm, and 75 mm immersion groups, respectively. This corresponds to reductions of 88.9%, 92.6%, and 100% relative to the non-immersed baseline.

claims.claim: No.10 transformer oil has a relatively low flash point and poses a fire risk compared to dimethyl silicone oil, which limits No.10 transformer oil's large-scale application for LIB thermal management.
claims.quote: No.10 transformer oil has a relatively low flash point and poses a fire risk, which limits its large-scale application for the LIBs thermal management.

claims.claim: HF concentration in the No.15 hydraulic oil-immersed group gradually declined after reaching its peak because HF was neutralized by alkaline additives (phosphate or amine-based inhibitors) in the hydraulic oil upon contact after safety valve opening.
claims.quote: HF concentration in the No.15 hydraulic oil-immersed group gradually reached its peak and subsequently declined. This phenomenon is mainly attributed to the fact that when the safety valve opens, HF contacts the No.15 hydraulic oil and is neutralized by its alkaline additives (No.15 hydraulic oil commonly contains phosphate or amine-based inhibitors)

claims.claim: Transformer oil and hydraulic oil are prone to thermal cracking at elevated temperatures and produce flammable products such as low-molecular-weight alkanes, alkenes, H2, and CO, which significantly increase the flammable gas load in the vapor phase during LIB thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the transformer oil and hydraulic oil are prone to thermal cracking at elevated temperatures and produce flammable products such as low-molecular-weight alkanes, alkenes, H2, and CO, which significantly increase the flammable gas load in the vapor phase

claims.claim: The gas suppression ranking of three immersion liquids (fully immersed at 75 mm) compared to non-immersed group was: EX > H2 > CO > HF > CO2.
claims.quote: In three immersed groups, the gas suppression ranking: EX >H2 > CO > HF > CO2.

claims.claim: The convection heat transfer coefficients of dimethyl silicone oil, No.15 hydraulic oil, and No.10 transformer oil are approximately 100 W/(m2·K), 80 W/(m2·K), and 210 W/(m2·K), respectively.
claims.quote: the convection heat transfer coefficients of dimethyl silicone oil, No.15 hydraulic oil, and No.10 transformer oil are approximately 100 W/(m2⋅K), 80 W/(m2⋅K), and 210 W/(m2⋅K), respectively.

claims.claim: The peak HF concentration in the non-immersed group (2.6 ppm) exceeded the maximum permissible concentration of 2.45 ppm, while all three immersed groups produced peak HF concentrations (0.4–0.6 ppm) below the maximum allowable concentration.
claims.quote: The maximum permissible concentration of HF is 2.45 ppm... The peak concentration of HF released by LIBs under experimental conditions of #1 to #4 was 2.6 ppm, 0.4 ppm, 0.4 ppm, and 0.6 ppm, respectively. The maximum HF concentration in non-immersed group was higher than the maximum allowable concentration... all of which was lower than the maximum allowable concentration.

claims.claim: Future work is needed to validate immersion cooling findings for diverse cell types (e.g., NCM, NCA, soft pack) and at module and pack level configurations, as the current study was conducted only on 26700 LFP cylindrical cells.
claims.quote: In the future, it is necessary to address these system-level issues and validate the approach for diverse cell types (e.g., NCM, NCA, soft pack) and real-world conditions to enable broad adoption. In addition, future work will expand this research to module and pack level configurations

claims.claim: The NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite pouch cell shows 83% capacity retention after 600 cycles at 1C under 45°C, compared to sharp attenuation of pristine NCM under the same conditions.
claims.quote: Under 45 ℃, the NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite pouch cell shows excellent 83% capacity retention after 600 cycles at 1C, which compared to the sharp attenuation of pristine NCM.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway temperature (TTR) of the NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite cell is delayed by 193°C compared to the pristine NCM||graphite cell.
claims.quote: NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite cell could pass the nail penetration tests and the thermal runaway temperature (TTR) delayed by 193℃, which proved the double layer design has better safety performance.

claims.claim: TTR for pristine NCM||graphite cell is 154°C, while TTR for NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite cell is 193°C higher, indicating better thermal safety for the double layer design.
claims.quote: TTR for pristine NCM||graphite and NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite cells are 154 ◦C and 193 ◦C (rate at 1 ◦C min−1). The higher TTR for NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite indicates better thermal safety.

claims.claim: The onset temperature (Tonset) of NCM||graphite cell is 85°C, which is lower than 90°C of NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite, indicating more stable SEI film formed on the double layer cathode.
claims.quote: The onset temperature (Tonset) of NCM||graphite cell is 85 ◦C, which is lower than 90 ◦C of NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite (Fig. 6a and b), indicating more stable SEI film formed on double layer cathode.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the pristine NCM||graphite cell shorted and combusted upon nail penetration, while the NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite pouch cell showed only voltage jitter and passed the test with no fire.
claims.quote: The NCM||graphite cell shorted and quickly combusted at the moment of puncture. However, the NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite pouch cell just has voltage jitter, but safely pass detection with no fire evident.

claims.claim: When coolant flow rate increases from 0.009 m/s to 0.029 m/s, the thermal runaway propagation time through the cooling plate extends by 1.84 times (from 17.8s to 32.9s).
claims.quote: when the flow rate of coolant is 0.009 m/s, the TR propagation time through the cooling plate is 17.8s, and when the flow rate of the coolant increases to 0.029 m/s, the TR propagation time through the cooling plate becomes 32.9s, which extends by 1.84 times.

claims.claim: At coolant flow speed of 0.009 m/s, all batteries in the pack undergo thermal runaway, whereas at flow speeds of 0.013 m/s or higher, batteries on the far side of the cooling plate do not trigger thermal runaway.
claims.quote: when the inlet flow speed is 0.009 m/s, all batteries in pack go through TR. This is because the relatively low speed fluid has limited heat dispassion ability, resulting in more heat transfer to the other side of cooling plate, and finally leading to the TR of battery1 to battery5. In the case of flow speed of 0.013 m/s or larger, coolant takes away more heat, and the heat transfers though cooling plate is not enough to trigger TR of batteries in the other side.

claims.claim: Decreasing CPCM thermal conductivity from 6 W/(m·K) to 0.5 W/(m·K) increases total thermal runaway propagation time from 23.5s to 112.7s.
claims.quote: when the thermal conductivity is 6 W/(m⋅K), the total TR time is 23.5s, and when the thermal conductivity is decreased to 0.5 W/(m⋅K), the total TR time is 112.7s.

claims.claim: When CPCM thermal conductivity is 0.5 W/(m·K), the maximum temperature of CPCM around the thermal runaway battery reaches 750.7 K with a large temperature gradient, whereas at 6 W/(m·K) the maximum CPCM temperature is only 552 K.
claims.quote: when the thermal conductivity is 0.5 W/(m⋅K), the maximum temperature of CPCM reaches 750.7 K, showing a large temperature gradient and challenging heat dissipation. When the thermal conductivity is high, heat transfer around the battery occurs rapidly, resulting in lower temperature gradients and a more uniform temperature distribution. For example, when the thermal conductivity is 6 W/(m⋅K), the maximum temperature of the CPCM around battery10 is only 552 K.

claims.claim: When coolant channel number in the cooling plate changes from 5 to 1, the thermal runaway propagation time through the cooling plate changes only from 24.7s to 26.2s, indicating that channel number has minimal influence on total thermal runaway propagation time.
claims.quote: When the number of coolant channel is 1, the thermal runaway propagation time through the liquid cooling plate is 26.2s. And when the number of coolant channel is 5, the thermal runaway propagation time through the liquid cooling plate is 24.7s.

claims.claim: The threshold temperature for battery thermal runaway is 120°C, and a BTMS is required to maintain battery temperature below this value to prevent fire risk under extreme conditions.
claims.quote: a battery thermal management system (BTMS) is required to maintain the battery temperature below 120 °C, which is the threshold temperature for thermal runaway, to prevent the risk of fire under extreme conditions

claims.claim: During normal operations, Li-ion batteries require a BTMS to maintain battery temperature below 50°C to avoid problems such as capacity loss.
claims.quote: Li-ion batteries also require a BTMS to maintain the battery temperature below 50 °C during normal operations to avoid problems such as capacity loss

claims.claim: The latent heat densities of previously studied PCMs (~150 kJ/kg) are far too low to store the large amount of heat (~880 kJ/kg) released during battery thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The latent heat densities (~150 kJ/kg) of previously studied PCMs are far too low to store the large amount of heat (~880 kJ/kg) released during battery thermal runaway

claims.claim: Once the latent heat of a PCM is exhausted, the high thermal conductivity of the PCM accelerates thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: Once the latent heat is exhausted, the high thermal conductivity of the PCMs would accelerate thermal runaway propagation

claims.claim: The burning of a flammable PCM introduces additional heat into the battery system, further intensifying thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The burning of the PCM could introduce additional heat into the battery system, further intensifying thermal runaway

claims.claim: A latent-failure accidental vehicle is defined as one where the faulty battery exhibits characteristic parameters or data traces distinct from normal cells over an extended period from the start of its service to one week before the accident.
claims.quote: If a vehicle's faulty battery exhibits characteristic parameters or data traces distinct from normal cells over an extended period from the start of its service to one week before the accident, the vehicle is classified as a 'latent-failure accidental vehicle.'

claims.claim: For sudden-failure accidental vehicles, the ARFD indicator alone is insufficient for cause inference, requiring a comprehensive review of full status information from the week before the accident including battery temperature, voltage, and insulation resistance.
claims.quote: for sudden failure vehicles, ARFD alone is insufficient to analyze the cause of failure. According to the definition of sudden failure, inferring the cause of these accidents requires data from the week prior to the accident, including charging, discharging, and resting data.

claims.claim: Continuous low cell voltage may lead to lithium deposition on the anode, causing internal short circuits and redox reactions with the electrolyte, thereby triggering battery thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Continuous low voltage may lead to lithium deposition on the anode, causing internal short circuits (ISC) and redox reactions with the electrolyte, thereby triggering battery thermal runaway.

claims.claim: In accidental vehicle 26, abnormal temperature spikes of up to 212°C lasting less than 10 seconds and insulation resistance drops occurred non-simultaneously, with no concurrent voltage anomalies, leading to the conclusion that the cause was external short circuit from cable damage rather than internal short circuit.
claims.quote: the battery pack experiences seven abnormal high temperatures of up to 212°C, the duration of which is less than 10 s... after data alignment, it is found that the temperature rises and insulation resistance drops do not simultaneously occur and there are no voltage anomalies during the same period.

claims.claim: According to Chinese national standard GB/T 18384, insulation resistance must be higher than 500 kΩ to ensure vehicle safety.
claims.quote: According to the Chinese national standard (GB/T 18384), the insulation resistance should be higher than 500 kΩ to ensure vehicle safety.

claims.claim: Within the oven temperature range of 150°C to 250°C, LFP batteries invariably undergo venting, with the venting force ranging from 4000 N to 6422 N.
claims.quote: within the oven temperature range of 150 C to 250 C, the LIBs invariably undergo venting, with the venting force ranging from 4000 N to 6422 N.

claims.claim: Internal short circuit and thermal runaway events occur once the oven temperature exceeds 180°C, with corresponding TR trigger temperatures of approximately 232°C.
claims.quote: An Internal short circuit and TR events occur once the oven temperature exceeds 180 C, and the corresponding TR trigger temperatures are approximately 232 C.

claims.claim: The lead time for expansion force warning signals decreases with increasing oven temperature in batteries undergoing thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the lead time for expansion force warning signals decreases with increasing oven temperature in batteries undergoing TR.

claims.claim: At an oven temperature of 180°C, the time interval from force anomaly detection to TR onset is 10,318 s, allowing early warning approximately 3 hours in advance.
claims.quote: at an oven temperature of 180 C, the time span from the detection of an expansion force anomaly (F > 1300 N) to the onset of TR is 10,318 s. This allows for the issuance of an early warning for TR about 3 h in advance.

claims.claim: The time interval from force anomaly to TR trigger reaches a maximum of 11,032 s at 180°C oven temperature and is reduced to a minimum of 3758 s at 250°C oven temperature.
claims.quote: the time interval reaches its maximum of 11,032 s at an oven temperature of 180 C, but it is reduced to a minimum of 3758 s at an oven temperature of 250 C.

claims.claim: A convective heat transfer coefficient exceeding 40 W/m2-K is capable of entirely suppressing thermal runaway propagation in densely packed batteries under 2C-rate discharge conditions.
claims.quote: under 2C-rate discharge conditions, a convective environment with a heat dissipation coefficient exceeding 40 W/m2-K (corresponding to a velocity of 1 m/s in this study) can entirely suppress TRP.

claims.claim: Increasing ambient pressure from 1 bar to 2 bar slowed thermal runaway propagation time from 634 s to 670 s, and completely inhibited propagation at 3 bar, under 3C discharge rate and 0.5 m/s air flow speed.
claims.quote: the TRP time slowed from 634 s at 1 bar to 670 s at 2 bar, and was completely inhibited at a pressure of 3 bar. This is attributed to the increase in fluid density with ambient pressure, leading to higher heat transfer capability.

claims.claim: When RTRP falls below 70%, thermal runaway cannot propagate to adjacent cells, and RTRP below 50% is recommended for high-safety design requirements.
claims.quote: when RTRP < 70 %, there is no propagation of flames to neighboring cells. However, it is preferable for RTRP to remain below 50 % for enhanced battery module safety.

claims.claim: Nitrogen exhibited superior heat dissipation capabilities compared to air and argon gas, while argon gas exhibited the lowest cooling effectiveness primarily due to its lower heat capacity despite having higher density.
claims.quote: the cooling system using argon gas exhibited the lowest effectiveness compared to air and nitrogen, primarily due to its lower heat capacity. Despite argon gas having higher density, its lower heat capacity exerts a more significant influence on the cooling rate. Nitrogen, on the other hand, exhibits superior capabilities in reducing the RTRP.

claims.claim: SEI decomposition began at 74°C, self-heating commenced at 145°C, thermal runaway was triggered at 166°C, and peak temperature reached 804°C in simulations of LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 cathode batteries under thermal ramp conditions.
claims.quote: the first exothermic chain reaction, SEI decomposition, was observed at 74°C. However, the temperature increase in the cell was not immediate. Self-heating commenced at 145°C, triggering TR at 166°C, reaching a peak temperature of 804°C.

claims.claim: When a level-1 warning was issued and overcharging ceased, the maximum battery temperature reached only 28.4°C, no pressure change was observed, and no damage occurred to the battery.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature of only 28.4 ◦C indicates that the reaction heat produced inside the battery remains normal at this stage. No change was observed in the pressure parameters during the entire process and no damage occurred to the battery.

claims.claim: When a level-2 warning was issued and overcharging ceased, the maximum battery temperature reached 41.5°C, and the pressure parameters returned to normal after the first surge, indicating minimal battery deformation with subsequent automatic recovery.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature was higher than that associated with the first-level warning at 41.5 ◦C, indicating that the reaction heat production inside the battery had begun to intensify, but it was still controllable. As the overcharging ceased, the pressure parameters began to decrease gradually after the first surge and slowly returned to normal, indicating minimal battery deformation with subsequent automatic recovery.

claims.claim: When a level-4 warning was issued and overcharging ceased, thermal runaway could not be prevented, and the maximum battery temperature reached 705.2°C.
claims.quote: When the pressure spikes for the second time; that is, when a level-four warning is issued, overcharging ceases and the subsequent battery voltage drops abruptly. However, after a short period, the battery experienced thermal runaway, with an immediate voltage drop to zero. The temperature continues to rise after overcharging ceases until thermal runaway occurs, and the maximum temperature reaches 705.2 ◦C.

claims.claim: The first characteristic pressure parameter (Pc1) occurs at approximately 9.75% SOC before thermal runaway, and the third characteristic pressure (Pc3) occurs at approximately 2.53% SOC before thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The occurrence time of the second group of characteristic parameters was approximately 10 % SOC before thermal runaway. Pc1 was used as the warning parameter at this point... The fourth group of characteristic parameters appeared at approximately 2.5 % SOC. Pc3 was used as the warning parameter

claims.claim: The escape time prediction errors for all four warning levels were within 20 seconds, with level-1 error at 16.56 s, level-2 at 11.52 s, level-3 at 11.88 s, and level-4 at 10.08 s.
claims.quote: The escape time errors were 16.56 s, 11.52 s, and 11.88 s respectively, all within 20 s for each level.

claims.claim: At a bending angle of 60° in a fully charged state (100% SOC), thermal runaway is triggered in LiCoO2/graphite soft pack LIBs under three-point bending.
claims.quote: it was found that 60◦ is the critical angle for the TR of LIBs in a fully charged state

claims.claim: At 30% SOC and a 60° bending angle, thermal runaway did not occur and the temperature did not rise significantly.
claims.quote: When the SOC was in the 30% state, TR did not occur, and the temperature did not rise significantly.

claims.claim: With decreasing SOC at a fixed 60° bending angle, the time to thermal runaway increases and the maximum temperature of thermal runaway decreases, with the highest temperature at 40% SOC reaching only 248°C.
claims.quote: With the gradual decrease in the battery SOC, the time of the battery thermal runaway gradually increases, the maximum temperature of the battery thermal runaway gradually decreases, and the highest temperature of the battery with 40% SOC is only 248℃.

claims.claim: At a critical bending angle of approximately 135°, there exists a competing relationship where below this angle irreversible heat dominates, and above this angle ISC Joule heat becomes the primary heat source and total heat generated exceeds that of normal batteries.
claims.quote: When the bending degree falls below this critical point, the internal resistance of the battery increases, with irreversible heat playing a predominant role in heat generation. Conversely, as the bending degree surpasses this critical point, the battery's ISC resistance (RISC) decreases, and heat generation from ISC becomes the primary contributor to the overall heat produced by the battery.

claims.claim: When ISC occurs due to bending, the solid-phase ion diffusion coefficient Ds decreases significantly by more than 80%, because rapid electron movement through the collector generates Joule heat and electrolytes cannot transport ions to the electrode surface in time.
claims.quote: When the bending degree continues to increase, the ion diffusion rate will significantly decrease above 80%. This is due to the rapid movement of electrons through the collector between the positive and negative electrodes when an ISC occurs, generating a large amount of Joule heat. Electrolytes are unable to transport ions to the electrode surface in time to participate in the electrochemical reaction.

claims.claim: Under external heating alone, thermal runaway of LiFeO4 battery occurs but combustion does not occur, as flammable and explosive gases are not ignited without an ignition source.
claims.quote: it can be concluded that thermal runaway of LiFeO4 battery used in this study does not cause combustion. Although there are lots of gases being generated, the flammable and explosive gases are not ignited without ignition sources.

claims.claim: Under external heating-ESC coupling abuse, the high-temperature electric conductor (temperature above 1000°C due to Joule heat) acts as the ignition source for flammable and explosive gases after the safety valve opens.
claims.quote: The temperature of the electric conductor is higher than 1000°C owing to Joule heat. The electric conductor is seen as ignition source. The explosive gas mixture is ignited.

claims.claim: External heating contributes much more to the accumulated heat of the battery before safety valve opening compared to the external short circuit under external heating-ESC coupling abuse.
claims.quote: The external heating contributes much more to accumulated heat of battery at the moment of safety valve open. This means that external heating plays the key role in the thermal runaway and combustion of battery under external heating-ESC coupling abuse.

claims.claim: With increasing external resistance, the maximum discharging current decreases, the combustion duration decreases and is less violent, but the exothermic side reactions increase after thermal runaway.
claims.quote: With the increase of external resistance, the combustion lasts shorter and is less violent, but the exothermic side reactions increase after thermal runaway.

claims.claim: With increasing triggering time of ESC, the combustion duration increases but the exothermic side reactions decrease after thermal runaway.
claims.quote: With the increase of triggering time of ESC, the combustion lasts longer, but the exothermic side reactions decrease after thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature during thermal runaway (TTR,max) reaches 427.3 °C for the 100% SOC battery, compared to only 317.1 °C for the 50% SOC battery, a difference of nearly 110 °C.
claims.quote: during thermal runaway, the maximum temperature (TTR, max) reaches 427.3 ℃ for the 100 % SOC battery, compared to only 317.1 ℃ for the 50 % SOC battery. Notably, the maximum temperature for the 100 % SOC battery exceeds that for the 50 % SOC battery by nearly 110 ℃.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway trigger time for the 100% SOC battery occurs nearly 1200 s earlier than for the 50% SOC battery.
claims.quote: the thermal runaway trigger time for the 100 % SOC is nearly 1200 s earlier than that for the 50 % SOC.

claims.claim: For the 0% SOC battery, thermal runaway does not occur even as the heating front temperature approaches 400 °C.
claims.quote: for the 0 % SOC battery, the temperature rise rate remains slow until Tf approaches 400 ℃, suggesting that thermal runaway does not occur.

claims.claim: The mass loss rate during thermal runaway peaks at 7.24 g/s for the 100% SOC battery and 2.72 g/s for the 50% SOC battery, indicating a higher gas release rate at higher SOC.
claims.quote: the mass loss rate curve for the 50 % SOC battery peaks at 2.72 g/s during thermal runaway, significantly lower than the peak value of 7.24 g/s observed for the 100 % SOC battery.

claims.claim: By identifying the transition point from exponential to linear stress growth, thermal runaway can be predicted approximately 2000 s in advance for the 50% SOC battery and approximately 1684 s in advance for the 100% SOC battery, compared to only within 20 s for voltage drop or temperature surge methods.
claims.quote: traditional methods, such as monitoring voltage drop or temperature surge, are only effective within 20 s before thermal runaway occurs, posing a high risk... By monitoring transition point of stress variation, thermal runaway can be predicted up to approximately 2000 s in advance.

claims.claim: At 100% SoC, the minimum laser energy required to induce thermal runaway in NMC811 18650 cells is 346.5 J, while at 50% SoC the required laser energy increases to 495 J.
claims.quote: It has been identified that the minimum laser energy required to induce the battery thermal runaway is 346.5 J in 100 % SoC cells. At 50 % SoC, the battery cells required 495 J of laser energy.

claims.claim: At 100% SoC with 5 s laser irradiation, the mean maximum air temperature was 400.1°C, whereas at 50% SoC under the same laser duration, the maximum air temperature varied from 285.5°C to 385.7°C.
claims.quote: at 100 % SoC with a laser irradiation of 5 s, the maximum air temperature has a mean value of 400.1 ◦C, whereas at 50 % SoC, it varies from 285.5 ◦C to 385.7 ◦C

claims.claim: Thermal runaway was not induced by 2.5 s laser irradiation regardless of SoC, and cells at 50% SoC did not enter thermal runaway after 3.5 s of laser irradiation.
claims.quote: The chamber pressure shows that the LITR do not induce thermal runaway when the irradiation time is 2,5 s independently on the SoC. The cells with 50 % SoC do not enter thermal runaway after 3.5 s of laser irradiation.

claims.claim: In laser irradiation tests, a sudden voltage drop preceded an abrupt increase in chamber pressure, associated with internal cell collapse and a subsequent chain reaction leading to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: in all cases, the sudden voltage drop precedes an abrupt increase of the chamber pressure, which may be associated to an internal cell collapse and a subsequent chain reaction that leads to the thermal runaway

claims.claim: All cases in which the transition from the turbulent vent stage to the particle vent stage was achieved ended in thermal runaway, making this transition a critical threshold for the chain reaction.
claims.quote: This is why all the cases in which the transition between the turbulent to the particle vent is achieved end in the thermal runaway phenomenon.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway was not triggered during 0.4 C and 0.5 C overcharge of NCM811 pouch cells, with surface peak temperatures of 94.14°C and 86.61°C respectively.
claims.quote: TR is not caused during the 0.4 C and 0.5 C overcharge process. This is attributed to the low charging current, which results in slower ohmic heating... Corresponding to 0.4 C and 0.5 C overcharge, the surface peak temperatures are 94.14°C and 86.61°C, respectively.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway was triggered under overcharge conditions at 0.8 C, 1 C, 1.5 C, 2 C, and 3 C, with TR trigger temperatures of 88.26°C, 93.71°C, 91.63°C, 93.82°C, and 89.56°C respectively.
claims.quote: the LIBs suffer TR under overcharge conditions at 0.8 C, 1 C, 1.5 C, 2 C, and 3 C... Corresponding to 0.8 C, 1 C, 1.5 C, 2 C, and 3 C overcharge, the TR trigger temperatures are 88.26°C, 93.71°C, 91.63°C, 93.82°C, and 89.56°C, respectively.

claims.claim: Exothermic side reactions account for 63.48% to 68.97% of total heat accumulation during overcharge-induced thermal runaway across overcharge rates of 0.8 C to 3 C.
claims.quote: The respective proportions of Qside are 68.97 %, 65.51 %, 63.6 %, 63.7 %, and 63.48 %, respectively. This indicates that exothermic side reactions are the primary contributions to TR.

claims.claim: The 140% SOC deeply delithiated NCM811 cathode begins thermal decomposition at 199.8°C and releases oxygen starting at 202.54°C, compared to 211.56°C decomposition onset and 215.48°C oxygen release for the 100% SOC cathode.
claims.quote: the deeply delithiated (140 % SOC) cathode decomposes at 199.8°C and exhibits two exothermic peaks at 221.42°C and 292.40°C, respectively, with ΔH of −64.72 J/g... the 100 % and 140 % SOC cathodes begin to release oxygen at 215.48°C and 202.54°C, respectively

claims.claim: At 140% SOC, the TR trigger temperature decreased to 187.26°C and peak temperature increased to 745.18°C, compared to 201.82°C trigger temperature and 723.69°C peak temperature at 100% SOC, when heated in a muffle furnace.
claims.quote: the TR trigger temperatures for 100 % and 140 % SOC are 201.82°C and 187.26°C, with the peak temperatures at 723.69°C and 745.18°C, respectively. At elevated charge states, the TR trigger temperature is reduced, and the peak temperature is elevated.
